And at least initially, they do get something: a modest, though nicely animated, title screen. Move over, Apple Presents Apple; this is DECmate Presents DECmate.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjMDNoSMsDBCmRxYUvL8JvCx_oU8yy_8XjR3ihoY16j9HhKEDHJ8KWlkEFMutm2nB4r0WLndCnU2K16Mmycwr896UqMvvmN2d0dAmxaHX3MzmCwi1mVPm5m4MJyDv4pVFcZqhoJSyJ6Gvfska6g_KqEJmECi6RXderUyY12BiOUu3ZUwir-p9NES0l08Cg/s320/PXL_20260420_234027856.PORTRAIT.ORIGINAL.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjMDNoSMsDBCmRxYUvL8JvCx_oU8yy_8XjR3ihoY16j9HhKEDHJ8KWlkEFMutm2nB4r0WLndCnU2K16Mmycwr896UqMvvmN2d0dAmxaHX3MzmCwi1mVPm5m4MJyDv4pVFcZqhoJSyJ6Gvfska6g_KqEJmECi6RXderUyY12BiOUu3ZUwir-p9NES0l08Cg/s4080/PXL_20260420_234027856.PORTRAIT.ORIGINAL.jpg)
Unfortunately, we can only get a little ways into the demonstration and tutorial ...
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiKGI9TuJMcdqCBIklWnp07aiFiicvlB4GZEMdtJdElbOiVQA63E8ajA5wIWN_tdjzt2wnBD06dazgu4A6RHF0cEciwDmskIH6fv5QSxTUpmAr9Bhhi0rHi84F5qjV6FmXT9-ECkyWuJjOW3Ypjjuod5nWMaDqjIW5aHAPQsiWaKjIFEZaUMiTiG8Pfek4/s320/PXL_20260420_234255249.PORTRAIT.ORIGINAL.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiKGI9TuJMcdqCBIklWnp07aiFiicvlB4GZEMdtJdElbOiVQA63E8ajA5wIWN_tdjzt2wnBD06dazgu4A6RHF0cEciwDmskIH6fv5QSxTUpmAr9Bhhi0rHi84F5qjV6FmXT9-ECkyWuJjOW3Ypjjuod5nWMaDqjIW5aHAPQsiWaKjIFEZaUMiTiG8Pfek4/s4080/PXL_20260420_234255249.PORTRAIT.ORIGINAL.jpg)
... before the system comes to a screeching halt. This is the DECmate II equivalent of a system error, complete with a full 6120 register dump, though the DECmate II was also the first of the line that could in some sense recover and even continue. On the DECmate VT278 and the VT78, you had no choice but to hard-reset.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEj8bgXcT1P5eQ5tqbDwusdFi_SbN5xUSjJa6y8sKu0kBy7lDE7EQ95UHxaQlSEJC6flWjb6AZZ7pcpAd_Lgl0vm0q4b3c1cnY88ejDF1LKwHaxkAUs_eZNRF0KDy56pG_ODhCmhzAJJzMPD3WdgX3bctimGWFW1cnlypt3l963DuciOkP2CnbEObwnNpLY/s320/PXL_20260420_234540762.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEj8bgXcT1P5eQ5tqbDwusdFi_SbN5xUSjJa6y8sKu0kBy7lDE7EQ95UHxaQlSEJC6flWjb6AZZ7pcpAd_Lgl0vm0q4b3c1cnY88ejDF1LKwHaxkAUs_eZNRF0KDy56pG_ODhCmhzAJJzMPD3WdgX3bctimGWFW1cnlypt3l963DuciOkP2CnbEObwnNpLY/s4080/PXL_20260420_234540762.jpg)
In this case the floppy disk appears to be corrupt enough or the disk drive defective enough (or the system shot enough?) that continuing didn't work, but we _can_ get into the slushware setup menu, where you can see the terminal and serial port settings. You can also boot another disk directly from here. Unlike typical serial terminals, because entering this menu might be unexpected by whatever the foreground program is, there is no key combination or button to press to immediately jump to it; the program has to _let_ you do so and/or poop its pants first.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjQbua5fbnq5cRfWmn4QUEz3hsOPqrLAwEykX-g2QiZ4pi8rc_nyK4nq81NLaie1u6rw3ZTxk5OHB09JMJENe0TTumGalwXcNfPiD6sj-ro0LDlEw4ZAxOjfVSflOeEsz1a4mgIYUi5DBHOf8yXRbdvaPFOScLf0supGbe-36fCiL2G-g-qWH4lJdvYQQ0/s320/PXL_20260420_234703501.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjQbua5fbnq5cRfWmn4QUEz3hsOPqrLAwEykX-g2QiZ4pi8rc_nyK4nq81NLaie1u6rw3ZTxk5OHB09JMJENe0TTumGalwXcNfPiD6sj-ro0LDlEw4ZAxOjfVSflOeEsz1a4mgIYUi5DBHOf8yXRbdvaPFOScLf0supGbe-36fCiL2G-g-qWH4lJdvYQQ0/s4080/PXL_20260420_234703501.jpg)
Since we got that error, trying the System Test disk seems the next logical step. We press Remove and are asked to switch floppies ...
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEht0bFk8BFFJdeWyYAAVyJnZLyD8ITZM0JWRMNdpIA3S_2_WqolMDSip-QbiTBFwqo6RSY7HgQZWgUkyNVHBCQwfPdPWTKrm_5Z0e2p-gV7vlicO5y_KisGS2VIp3_UgH6hCdOJQAvdrzCU2m3UnWMOysOfubAzo6xh1JH7SfYa-Lqwgm4m2Ench7-M_BE/s320/PXL_20260420_234637344.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEht0bFk8BFFJdeWyYAAVyJnZLyD8ITZM0JWRMNdpIA3S_2_WqolMDSip-QbiTBFwqo6RSY7HgQZWgUkyNVHBCQwfPdPWTKrm_5Z0e2p-gV7vlicO5y_KisGS2VIp3_UgH6hCdOJQAvdrzCU2m3UnWMOysOfubAzo6xh1JH7SfYa-Lqwgm4m2Ench7-M_BE/s4080/PXL_20260420_234637344.jpg)
... so we do, and press Do after we do so.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjCx2EPjqyvSaC7a6VHzaeO5flT8f3RkFxnIIzIhyphenhyphenGtX9u4jOYmIK0o8LTI1YCazqmlrAkuEe-FhP3dExsDQWt6LX8uKJbzUjMSGdUdHccRiizzSR-uXyL7Ya0b3KQgCTnCFVhVn7N-FbrTQ1z0I6ZfBtkXYvencRTkHBFpyW9n8MCoeFmIsMqqgRX_fCs/s320/PXL_20260420_234738165.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjCx2EPjqyvSaC7a6VHzaeO5flT8f3RkFxnIIzIhyphenhyphenGtX9u4jOYmIK0o8LTI1YCazqmlrAkuEe-FhP3dExsDQWt6LX8uKJbzUjMSGdUdHccRiizzSR-uXyL7Ya0b3KQgCTnCFVhVn7N-FbrTQ1z0I6ZfBtkXYvencRTkHBFpyW9n8MCoeFmIsMqqgRX_fCs/s4080/PXL_20260420_234738165.jpg)
No flashing disk error this time.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiB4KMrj3xAsf-UrBIA98QvXARxmxEWAGn8yUsY5DkLAmtsPqwkkEHuppL8h1rN9OrlEAlt6u_szy1k4CyDk0rk1KtpjQPlhQt9u1Z0uzB8RmdtwM_TdloBv4NrY4RW3VRDm7mwW-3coOrXXwBTBKXIWsQdnAhhcYlBqrDwWFXS5tU5f12_qKhwgIppstU/s320/PXL_20260420_234812447.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiB4KMrj3xAsf-UrBIA98QvXARxmxEWAGn8yUsY5DkLAmtsPqwkkEHuppL8h1rN9OrlEAlt6u_szy1k4CyDk0rk1KtpjQPlhQt9u1Z0uzB8RmdtwM_TdloBv4NrY4RW3VRDm7mwW-3coOrXXwBTBKXIWsQdnAhhcYlBqrDwWFXS5tU5f12_qKhwgIppstU/s4080/PXL_20260420_234812447.jpg)
The System Test disk is the nucleus of potentially an entire suite of self-tests and can also be used to format an attached hard disk. You'll note, however, that there is no option to format a _floppy_ disk, and that's because DEC explicitly didn't allow it for the DECmate II. In fact, they didn't even allow it for the DEC Professionals either, roiling the user base who rebelled when forced to buy preformatted RX50 media from DEC or one of their overpriced tame vendors. Officially only later versions of CP/M and MS-DOS for the Rainbow could do this, and some users ended up buying Rainbows expressly for that purpose. We'll run the system test and see what's what with the hardware. Since this requires two floppies to test both drives, I put the System Overview disk into the second drive (remember, it goes upside down into the bottom slot).
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEj_2vsaX_jo7vM9MysiUrZQRZx7x1vfnzdnZ_AZA8E6ZO5LtDzzb6ULL4TMjYVZDbNLlqwt33R_Wu6aQ4RPqCaAniXr2cc5Ju6na6mLakcM7EK4n-_1b7BTQoW2Mg5TMyxe9NStNlkPAMJoazIdFH3l8AjPh0pUpkcJXjwwwD9odhm8iQfTyamUDIF6Lk4/s320/PXL_20260420_235221313.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEj_2vsaX_jo7vM9MysiUrZQRZx7x1vfnzdnZ_AZA8E6ZO5LtDzzb6ULL4TMjYVZDbNLlqwt33R_Wu6aQ4RPqCaAniXr2cc5Ju6na6mLakcM7EK4n-_1b7BTQoW2Mg5TMyxe9NStNlkPAMJoazIdFH3l8AjPh0pUpkcJXjwwwD9odhm8iQfTyamUDIF6Lk4/s4080/PXL_20260420_235221313.jpg)
The system board fortunately checks out, but the drives don't, and a thorough grinding with a 5.25" cleaning diskette didn't make a difference either. However, the test program is strictly pass-fail, much like my college grade point average, and there isn't enough detail to say if the floppy disk or the disk drive (or both) is to blame.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEh0Dy8Sy8NfaAdfkdR3VmxC36jTAeG3rCLZKX70Ts5hkbGVoRA8c196D0lTWi0f8pFZg0kQ3rYSx7aTR2lk_KiRiKqWNh_4adJjrNRrGK1iSQwXIPafWfw6DHEmX9APAK9NPecQWErL3heyxMSFAD5cchCoR2KodLizJHddrS0qzpBFSRG_bUd_CYvW07Y/s320/PXL_20260420_235448549.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEh0Dy8Sy8NfaAdfkdR3VmxC36jTAeG3rCLZKX70Ts5hkbGVoRA8c196D0lTWi0f8pFZg0kQ3rYSx7aTR2lk_KiRiKqWNh_4adJjrNRrGK1iSQwXIPafWfw6DHEmX9APAK9NPecQWErL3heyxMSFAD5cchCoR2KodLizJHddrS0qzpBFSRG_bUd_CYvW07Y/s4080/PXL_20260420_235448549.jpg)
For a more thorough test, which will hopefully generate a disk image as a useful side effect that we can verify, let's pull out the RX50 and try driving it manually with a [Greaseweazle controller](https://github.com/keirf/greaseweazle). The drive uses a regular 34-pin Shugart floppy connector to the logic board, so we just pull the power connector and disconnect the floppy interface from the top, then depress the front lock tab and slide it out.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjU07QIpMJZ3_S3VOin7S5ORcdDAAFKtwDbSxxTvNhRUusVRjmSgmZCQmpEWjt9-OR4IhccsYWLWapz2oa7XWNvh0ZcpJ-tpARGjEpzNvYEYtFmSIYTc26fZ5m_FKwSPogc1ye641D3Nd2MQiTAaCSKGr7tFkTHuP7msrOdP7Xka-Jdw8aVnBnaEa3ofVA/s320/PXL_20260420_235635405.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjU07QIpMJZ3_S3VOin7S5ORcdDAAFKtwDbSxxTvNhRUusVRjmSgmZCQmpEWjt9-OR4IhccsYWLWapz2oa7XWNvh0ZcpJ-tpARGjEpzNvYEYtFmSIYTc26fZ5m_FKwSPogc1ye641D3Nd2MQiTAaCSKGr7tFkTHuP7msrOdP7Xka-Jdw8aVnBnaEa3ofVA/s4080/PXL_20260420_235635405.jpg)
Unfortunately the DECmate's own floppy cable will not connect to a Greaseweazle; it has a key pin. Since we're already considering a solid state floppy emulator, let's just take the cable out entirely. It is secured to the chassis by a metal tab here, back by the main power supply cable ...
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEh5lwziRdgFJ19ama5ehWz9MA2dv3SMJgQvorwc3NBiNGYNv70l4gANSu_geS5REm_llbJRCxjz2fglqJBRNuj_RAoKuBEIOZdtqb3jMzNzAZkVKp82En_8_VzSe20taMSyTHA0WoFy6iCL43KEMFiVC_Nsms5_Y8en0mc-KZDQPcKLLXPNuRxhbSTITyQ/s320/PXL_20260420_235728027.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEh5lwziRdgFJ19ama5ehWz9MA2dv3SMJgQvorwc3NBiNGYNv70l4gANSu_geS5REm_llbJRCxjz2fglqJBRNuj_RAoKuBEIOZdtqb3jMzNzAZkVKp82En_8_VzSe20taMSyTHA0WoFy6iCL43KEMFiVC_Nsms5_Y8en0mc-KZDQPcKLLXPNuRxhbSTITyQ/s4080/PXL_20260420_235728027.jpg)
... and here, on top of the power supply itself where the Molex power connectors exit. We slide the lugs off the tabs and this will then free the cable fully.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEj5mtrjDgk92yoiUMgvuyKR5BWTSbNLNBR6K1PBYVsXpaNpugi6-sp6PGS-oc2MhKgTezgb-xTF3A51Mwrn9cvXVhPAP39PwbFh76tNQPYfSsadiNv9Ao4fRXc27wTxT-iKTf9BH6P8PE0asinu4I2RmiRASi72csyQ4mynQEhDIymrtJ25hzgeu1T2sAk/s320/PXL_20260421_164939244.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEj5mtrjDgk92yoiUMgvuyKR5BWTSbNLNBR6K1PBYVsXpaNpugi6-sp6PGS-oc2MhKgTezgb-xTF3A51Mwrn9cvXVhPAP39PwbFh76tNQPYfSsadiNv9Ao4fRXc27wTxT-iKTf9BH6P8PE0asinu4I2RmiRASi72csyQ4mynQEhDIymrtJ25hzgeu1T2sAk/s4080/PXL_20260421_164939244.jpg)
Next, the drive itself. There are two ways you can connect an RX50 directly to a Greaseweazle. The simplest is to use a standard twisted PC floppy cable, but plug the _twisted_ end in (as you face the unit, pin 1 is on the left), which also gives you the most length. I set it up this way initially for testing purposes.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjYksUAJWO6S9ykvhe8OOj6LSdJxMvQ1vAEdSEOsIwyLWe4c293Qu4_sZkzfRCRpu721_JdDiHzRk4ZNiABntNwA4uOPczUMvfdAP5r3ZrbmNGNCna84sCLaBEPkjj6Nf4ElEMD9WMZadUmh4Z5Kuy8eK7rHA5BWq_uMbeHJuVcXHtlTLnTJnM7kSVXWRY/s320/PXL_20260421_165004658.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjYksUAJWO6S9ykvhe8OOj6LSdJxMvQ1vAEdSEOsIwyLWe4c293Qu4_sZkzfRCRpu721_JdDiHzRk4ZNiABntNwA4uOPczUMvfdAP5r3ZrbmNGNCna84sCLaBEPkjj6Nf4ElEMD9WMZadUmh4Z5Kuy8eK7rHA5BWq_uMbeHJuVcXHtlTLnTJnM7kSVXWRY/s4080/PXL_20260421_165004658.jpg)
In this configuration only the lower drive is active, which the Greaseweazle will see as drive A, so you'll need to insert any floppies upside down as usual. However, should you need both drives active, you can connect a straight-thru floppy cable just like the DECmate II's (or the first part of a PC twisted cable before the twist, if you don't mind it being shorter), after which the top drive will also be available to the Greaseweazle as drive B. Yes, this is different from how the DECmate itself enumerates the drives, but we can cope with complexity, no?
A quick speed check before we begin to verify the drive responds and the interface is operating.
``` % gw rpm Rate: 300.629 rpm ; Period: 199.581 ms % gw rpm --drive b Rate: 300.682 rpm ; Period: 199.577 ms
```
Now to attempt some reads. I threw together a quick little `diskdef` to make this process easier:
``` % cat rx50.diskdef disk rx50 cyls = 80 heads = 1 tracks * ibm.mfm secs = 10 bps = 512 rate = 250 end end
```
Here we have defined the RX50 format as a single-sided MFM floppy with 80 tracks, 10 sectors per track, 512 bytes per sector and a data rate of 250kHz (i.e., double density). This will generate a 400K image file.
Unfortunately both disks had multiple bad sectors. Some were more readable on several tries, and the lower drive was more consistent, but other sectors seemed completely blown. As the error pattern was generally the same between both drives, I concluded the floppy disks themselves were most likely shot. Here's the best read of the test disk:
``` % gw read --diskdefs=rx50.diskdef --format=rx50 testdisk.img Reading c=0-79:h=0 revs=2 Format rx50 T0.0: IBM MFM (10/10 sectors) from Raw Flux (86384 flux in 399.75ms) T1.0: IBM MFM (10/10 Found 749 sectors of 800 (93%) % ls -l testrx50.img -rw-r--r-- 1 censored staff 409600 Apr 21 09:58 testdisk.img
```
You can see several of the tracks from 70 on up were kaput, but most of the rest of the disk was fine, and tracks 0, 1, 78 and 79 in particular were clean. As this particular disk only has basic system tests on it, it doesn't appear we ever encountered those bad tracks during our usage. On the other hand, here's the best read of the overview disk:
``` % gw read --diskdefs=rx50.diskdef --format=rx50 overview.img Reading c=0-79:h=0 revs=2 Format rx50 T0.0: IBM MFM (10/10 sectors) from Raw Flux (86508 flux in 399.82ms) T1.0: IBM MFM (9/10 sectors) from Raw Flux (83769 flux in 399.91ms) [...] Cyl-> 0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 H. S: 01234567890123456789012345678901234567890123456789012345678901234567890123456789 0. 0: ................................................................................ 0. 1: ...X............................................................................ 0. 2: .X.............................................................................. 0. 3: ...X............................................................................ 0. 4: ................................................................................ 0. 5: ................................................................................ 0. 6: ...X............................................................................ 0. 7: .......................................................................X........ 0. 8: ...X............................................................................ 0. 9: .......................................................................X........ Found 793 sectors of 800 (99%) % ls -l overview.img -rw-r--r-- 1 censored staff 409600 Apr 21 09:51 overview.img
```
While only a few sectors read bad, those errors early on in tracks 1 and particularly 3 likely corrupted part of the main program, which in turn almost certainly triggered the program halt we saw.
Before conceding defeat, though, I wanted to compare the sectors that were readable with any other sector dumps of these disks to see if they were an unusual version or not. There are a few archives of DECmate-compatible software such as [ibiblio](https://www.ibiblio.org/pub/academic/computer-science/history/pdp-8/diskimages/rx50/). The ibilio disk images are in [Teledisk](http://dunfield.classiccmp.org/img42841/teledisk.htm) format, which we could potentially use to remaster our floppies, but here we simply want to turn these Teledisk images into sector dumps as well so that we can compare and/or splice. [`wteledsk`](https://github.com/jmechnich/wteledsk) can do that: it will display the metadata in the Teledisk image first, then extract out the sector data. I tried it on ibiblio's version of the system test desk.
``` % wteledsk testdisk/TESTDISK.TD0 -oTESTDISK.IMG
BL-HV86A-MV 075591 DM SYSTEM TEST DISKETTE V4.5
COPR (c) 1986 DIGITAL EQUIP. CORP.
created June 01, 1994 17:10:41 string len 0x70 start variable 0x86 = 0x70 with 8 NULs file length 0x35a88 = 219784
Normal EOF on track 79 #sectors in track record = 0xff final file position 0x35a88 => this is EOF parsed 80 logical tracks, 800 sectors with data found 0 skipped sectors and 0 repeated (ignored)
max data sectors/track 10
```
The metadata and copyright date suggests this is a somewhat later disk from the DECmate II's successor model, unimaginatively called the DECmate III (or possibly the III+), which I'll talk about at the end when we finish the story. This disk is a bit differently organized and wouldn't be a good source for our missing sectors. However, the Internet Archive has [an image of our exact disk](https://archive.org/details/decmate-ii-system-test-diskette) in both [ImageDisk format](https://imagedisk.com/) and as a 400K raw sector dump. It also has [the same version of the Overview](https://archive.org/details/decmate-ii-system-overview-Diskette) and some other [training software](https://archive.org/search?query=Decmate&tab=all) examples. We could easily remaster our floppies with those, and it is possible to use a PC 5.25" with certain software packages to do so. (Or even format them — just don't tell DEC.)
But, now that we know we have exact images of our otherwise iffy disks, and there are more disks to be had out there that we can play with, I think it's time to bring on the Goteks.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEj5c2whp1FNe9EL8bZ91buMHl5dSI569OOQiuvcDudQpnYHKC5jCt2S70PYY31sh1ydMSVojIkrwajVuIcRE76i0RaLEsXQwoAiKguIq2SyzL0AC_Lq8qqpTd9C6XxYb9H6bC6MhcPiQdMjMfYMKYhkfDvAOeGWWJdwP-SFHSasrWpKnlsVpfr8Y3fbutE/s320/PXL_20260421_173911119.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEj5c2whp1FNe9EL8bZ91buMHl5dSI569OOQiuvcDudQpnYHKC5jCt2S70PYY31sh1ydMSVojIkrwajVuIcRE76i0RaLEsXQwoAiKguIq2SyzL0AC_Lq8qqpTd9C6XxYb9H6bC6MhcPiQdMjMfYMKYhkfDvAOeGWWJdwP-SFHSasrWpKnlsVpfr8Y3fbutE/s4080/PXL_20260421_173911119.jpg)
I cobbled together this franken-solid-state-RX50 out of a new-style rotary encoder Gotek floppy emulator and an old button-based one I still had on the shelf. Since I figured I would be primarily using the second drive only as a data disk, I decided I'd use the older unit to just flip between a couple scratch volumes. Both have [FlashFloppy firmware](https://github.com/keirf/flashfloppy).
Because of the RX50's unusual construction and common motor-stepper assembly, whether you can successfully swap a pair of Goteks for an RX50 in any arbitrary DEC machine appears to depend greatly on the system's firmware. The DECmate II is atypical in that it usually works fine. This Gotek sandwich was originally intended for my DEC Professional 380, but the POST sequence would complain about it every time, and I've yet to get it working. On the other hand, there are people using them (apparently with different DEC Pro floppy controller cards) where they operate with no difficulty. One theory is that the more recalcitrant systems assume that both drives step in sync, as they would on a real RX50, and there is at least one attempt at [modified FlashFloppy firmware](https://forum.vcfed.org/index.php?threads/dec-rainbow-pc100a-replacing-bad-rx50-with-dual-gotek-drives-strange-behavior.1257321/) to duplicate that behaviour. Even later DECmate III systems can be troublesome.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEho6FPCyns5WZ0UGSsCsBR1ZieBE99wJZNjZf1dj5V_tzcWRSuNr7mGF44l-E1TxLmK_NQsq6QPyVn_rpfu5bRfWhj-cTAtYEX656_VtcJWDK1N0rj3zpIiPFWi62mNZe7qtohICjADNxuA8DtH4m_sFPR2Qt5H5PhWh9neWBOd_5U1K3x5x6SdbL8Lys4/s320/PXL_20260421_173929492.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEho6FPCyns5WZ0UGSsCsBR1ZieBE99wJZNjZf1dj5V_tzcWRSuNr7mGF44l-E1TxLmK_NQsq6QPyVn_rpfu5bRfWhj-cTAtYEX656_VtcJWDK1N0rj3zpIiPFWi62mNZe7qtohICjADNxuA8DtH4m_sFPR2Qt5H5PhWh9neWBOd_5U1K3x5x6SdbL8Lys4/s4080/PXL_20260421_173929492.jpg)
Since the, uh, superstructure will all be hidden inside the case anyway, I built it out of two 3.5"-to-5.25" bay converters and stuck little rubber standoffs between then to act as spacers, then taped it all together.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgK-zYDvD3BdV2MeJV6zk1Dutz__iEdL6EwrAeTF1USBeA0XnPMG7bKHvpho0G2Zl3cYxz85tdV0Cx1GBGYgGlYTP1OVDkWEQcMCESvSu_0FTFngPiRD99v_8FZB9wQLzxecHPbGYWvfEslQXLfIX8NHwGPdNjsEq8AdPko_w2jHZs6u27hMh2UXQxHZ7Y/s320/PXL_20260421_173950881.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgK-zYDvD3BdV2MeJV6zk1Dutz__iEdL6EwrAeTF1USBeA0XnPMG7bKHvpho0G2Zl3cYxz85tdV0Cx1GBGYgGlYTP1OVDkWEQcMCESvSu_0FTFngPiRD99v_8FZB9wQLzxecHPbGYWvfEslQXLfIX8NHwGPdNjsEq8AdPko_w2jHZs6u27hMh2UXQxHZ7Y/s4080/PXL_20260421_173950881.jpg)
The floppy cable is an ordinary 34-pin PC floppy cable with a twist, with the top drive connected after the twist and jumpered to MO (no S0 or S1 jumper). The bottom drive is connected first before the twist. A Y-cable for power completes the connections.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgqpycuY4wQNv62sNGNAGVwZQ_tQmoMICdKHOnhjdQdaAhnqkKKbBmI_Z871fkYKnxXuGVEmwDMy8RuQteQW9zEI4MLiFDirDgZIhHSmFrHtNDW8-w_BoJfuBzoUXZuf9lJflRFt0akBLYT_HG9U3x5PTafrT5J6pMfuugTF-JQOSHzVU-3dkjfhac8GLY/s320/PXL_20260420_235844802.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgqpycuY4wQNv62sNGNAGVwZQ_tQmoMICdKHOnhjdQdaAhnqkKKbBmI_Z871fkYKnxXuGVEmwDMy8RuQteQW9zEI4MLiFDirDgZIhHSmFrHtNDW8-w_BoJfuBzoUXZuf9lJflRFt0akBLYT_HG9U3x5PTafrT5J6pMfuugTF-JQOSHzVU-3dkjfhac8GLY/s4080/PXL_20260420_235844802.jpg)
The whole assembly fits nearly perfectly where the RX50 did, though we'll install it in a proper sled once we've verified compatibility.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjM9B6Y6LFkOwOe8QOXQ13CKxhwwCjZvtyVbu7pXFw7usFM-21OTkp2iPhVlUpzqejRQEyjhdpt6N_9YYMIZK95MivKdGHo1Qk64kCRB4ghRwmTeDktBGYshTSNqkEvAir2oFfb1sOaj5Db62Gye9KgMfokNVWADFLh9J_QqVnYycu6rPvZkrsMAenGRjQ/s320/PXL_20260421_000839611.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjM9B6Y6LFkOwOe8QOXQ13CKxhwwCjZvtyVbu7pXFw7usFM-21OTkp2iPhVlUpzqejRQEyjhdpt6N_9YYMIZK95MivKdGHo1Qk64kCRB4ghRwmTeDktBGYshTSNqkEvAir2oFfb1sOaj5Db62Gye9KgMfokNVWADFLh9J_QqVnYycu6rPvZkrsMAenGRjQ/s4080/PXL_20260421_000839611.jpg)
The last things to do are to load some sample disk images and configure the FlashFloppy firmware for the interface connection. I put an image of the System Test disk on the top unit's USB stick and a blank RX50 image on the bottom's, then changed the following in [`FF.CFG`](https://github.com/keirf/flashfloppy/wiki/FF.CFG-Configuration-File) on each unit's stick:
``` interface = shugart host = dec pin02 = low pin34 = rdy
```
I also added a `ejected-on-startup = yes` for convenience to the top drive's configuration but that's personal preference, and powered everything up to read the settings in.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgCJ7Jr4ZI9-X3OHqRjt17wtyq2L7drzSxr9l7xurPLoYu1u7l8kyUroPH1BKAFneP-FsKhWUAhGu6GFNlvEflWs5IjMpZHV-vPRgl2SixLX-yoWbCcJgPczHtrcBATFjRRZ0QZVzVC4F0PA_CScdHJLEpOSqCWPa5qSNXXA9IE5X6KsNngtazZ9yljlnw/s320/PXL_20260421_000916494.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgCJ7Jr4ZI9-X3OHqRjt17wtyq2L7drzSxr9l7xurPLoYu1u7l8kyUroPH1BKAFneP-FsKhWUAhGu6GFNlvEflWs5IjMpZHV-vPRgl2SixLX-yoWbCcJgPczHtrcBATFjRRZ0QZVzVC4F0PA_CScdHJLEpOSqCWPa5qSNXXA9IE5X6KsNngtazZ9yljlnw/s4080/PXL_20260421_000916494.jpg)
Of course, it helps if you plug the Gotek sandwich into the computer's floppy interface as well, or you'll get an error code 32 when you try to start (the brief concomitant moment of panic is just a bonus). The DECmate II ROMs are not as nice as the DEC Pros about telling you where the problem is.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiZ7NqUvwuXP4_speuppu_P8FRHpH3pT2F7c1OLrpL4Lrmq2EsS8uAs0ns4So1lcaCnRE1tmek29Cj2emKkOKqoMGUp9VvNGhjrj5DM-OC8b4jwwDdSzzYmJGPi6rJyJtPV7ql70A25bxw5ukMcf27yyymidV-JDXLHVSs4EMQAXk0hPCnJJmouihVVlwk/s320/PXL_20260421_155758421.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiZ7NqUvwuXP4_speuppu_P8FRHpH3pT2F7c1OLrpL4Lrmq2EsS8uAs0ns4So1lcaCnRE1tmek29Cj2emKkOKqoMGUp9VvNGhjrj5DM-OC8b4jwwDdSzzYmJGPi6rJyJtPV7ql70A25bxw5ukMcf27yyymidV-JDXLHVSs4EMQAXk0hPCnJJmouihVVlwk/s4080/PXL_20260421_155758421.jpg)
It's booting!
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhkjuYY8p25SBp65kbebRX3GWwiVoR4D_D7bPUafH4ErnB3xTzaixG1N7vXXbghOiLXpqzSouVr6R-sulSe7o4trFAQB_q-Chynvxixs4xQhYGAIEVAgFNwcRnbs7RBAZ4RvRk_V3IsZmcqFklRgjno-kC9U08RWmmV8HknQ6QesTmCcyFDatd9Br779x4/s320/PXL_20260421_160355990.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhkjuYY8p25SBp65kbebRX3GWwiVoR4D_D7bPUafH4ErnB3xTzaixG1N7vXXbghOiLXpqzSouVr6R-sulSe7o4trFAQB_q-Chynvxixs4xQhYGAIEVAgFNwcRnbs7RBAZ4RvRk_V3IsZmcqFklRgjno-kC9U08RWmmV8HknQ6QesTmCcyFDatd9Br779x4/s4080/PXL_20260421_160355990.jpg)
We reach the menu successfully, not substantially faster but certainly no slower, to start the System Test again. Notice the track numbers counting up on the second drive during that test.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhuG-l1X3E_7mUk0cU6SJAEAcF7HB5pKr6N6whV0dDz6LiSJdLkpDz8R3qVW3LfPZDPcMeNYSG_9DAF3bAKS1bqpsXTerfnWaOEkGformWFRdYBFcHRIXqizdniNJjXUEgWcoZw3aoBcbgXtya8buWvRLWmNP8O6tzHh_k_qJoGU-hI8wgCw6wPvzHryG0/s320/PXL_20260421_160514329.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhuG-l1X3E_7mUk0cU6SJAEAcF7HB5pKr6N6whV0dDz6LiSJdLkpDz8R3qVW3LfPZDPcMeNYSG_9DAF3bAKS1bqpsXTerfnWaOEkGformWFRdYBFcHRIXqizdniNJjXUEgWcoZw3aoBcbgXtya8buWvRLWmNP8O6tzHh_k_qJoGU-hI8wgCw6wPvzHryG0/s4080/PXL_20260421_160514329.jpg)
Full pass — both Gotek units test "OK" to the DECmate II.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjaTMbMEMw7b37x1P5qPPI5TV_-oFaFZRppL4R7M4rqTvU9zHM9qjApjd6y01T9m819iacebZcLcXDdLSbZcJ0GYI9z68Yq4vJqZFjpKve7L4FCOSqICp0tZc8gsRsg9Y-TaXMmRnq8OvTp9R9SJs6jHtzmsSVtOF8SeqOw5xKBUbfviDgVmUxff35QvaQ/s320/PXL_20260421_210202374.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjaTMbMEMw7b37x1P5qPPI5TV_-oFaFZRppL4R7M4rqTvU9zHM9qjApjd6y01T9m819iacebZcLcXDdLSbZcJ0GYI9z68Yq4vJqZFjpKve7L4FCOSqICp0tZc8gsRsg9Y-TaXMmRnq8OvTp9R9SJs6jHtzmsSVtOF8SeqOw5xKBUbfviDgVmUxff35QvaQ/s4080/PXL_20260421_210202374.jpg)
To install it more securely, we will next take the sled off the RX50 and mount it on the Gotek sandwich. The four screws on the underside hold the sled on.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiMfTAs1zUBXryvAcQm5h4k1WM14Vzdte-bToAaiF7_WgxS7w9S643SBpSasu96QU3HPziZBp4YtO1tVsajyPdLxKGqnLHAVj7txWOIhD6ycJRQ56CNetNgCt4lgt4SfsnUXFvnyDE_xURiQUPGjBHOpSA2K1IN4ALsaX4WVl-Zn0eCEOelxSmqmxFZogQ/s320/PXL_20260421_210913884~2.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiMfTAs1zUBXryvAcQm5h4k1WM14Vzdte-bToAaiF7_WgxS7w9S643SBpSasu96QU3HPziZBp4YtO1tVsajyPdLxKGqnLHAVj7txWOIhD6ycJRQ56CNetNgCt4lgt4SfsnUXFvnyDE_xURiQUPGjBHOpSA2K1IN4ALsaX4WVl-Zn0eCEOelxSmqmxFZogQ/s4080/PXL_20260421_210913884~2.jpg)
However, these screws are a bit too big to fit the smaller mounting holes on the 5.25" bay converter, so I put the screws in the RX50 (in case I decide to put it in something else or use it as a second set of drives) and used a smaller set from my bag of tricks.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEj6SMP7TcfwEU6yICoCEXsGH-yMUMsVlAErwDSbI3SrU2qhZ2RUqXQPsBHmK1Xx34YT1ESw91pbzIBpFEYRJRmC_tOfffxh8UeQNtJUTLSyZE7jCpBScJWXDd5ZePTD_U4t6Jx2wOKj0NKNgsv7Q31dy6quLZjwes6r7fBoMbJ5ZBLm8f6FEOQauOXrlHM/s320/PXL_20260421_211050229.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEj6SMP7TcfwEU6yICoCEXsGH-yMUMsVlAErwDSbI3SrU2qhZ2RUqXQPsBHmK1Xx34YT1ESw91pbzIBpFEYRJRmC_tOfffxh8UeQNtJUTLSyZE7jCpBScJWXDd5ZePTD_U4t6Jx2wOKj0NKNgsv7Q31dy6quLZjwes6r7fBoMbJ5ZBLm8f6FEOQauOXrlHM/s4080/PXL_20260421_211050229.jpg)
This will now slide neatly along the rails, though the length of the sandwich does not quite match the RX50, so it won't be flush with the case opening. I dithered over how to fix this and eventually put a clip on the back of the sled. This ended up scratching the bottom surface under the sandwich, which I'm not quite happy about, but ...
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi71_M2Kt_ey3l64-Mw5pHqRkgeDJCkl9t46x9Hq-CEEz6ue6SU_e23yT52yUWCKGBiScVVHFVz2iTMWkK-QX35UqX3eJApld_-nbmM7296jsVBBKFCRdbo7Mz2gZfAd_hBYlZeFHCQnrzgWpQIRD675jv8kSHUu2dWDBCKCrWFpsZoUksf8Wk08urRbok/s320/PXL_20260421_212139006.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi71_M2Kt_ey3l64-Mw5pHqRkgeDJCkl9t46x9Hq-CEEz6ue6SU_e23yT52yUWCKGBiScVVHFVz2iTMWkK-QX35UqX3eJApld_-nbmM7296jsVBBKFCRdbo7Mz2gZfAd_hBYlZeFHCQnrzgWpQIRD675jv8kSHUu2dWDBCKCrWFpsZoUksf8Wk08urRbok/s4080/PXL_20260421_212139006.jpg)
... from the outside it looks nice and neat. I find the two-tone look kind of fun, actually. Remember that faceplates for the hard disks of this era were usually black as well, so this isn't a shocking computer fashion statement.
As a final test, I downloaded the raw image of the System Overview from the Internet Archive as linked above and put it on the top drive's stick.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjXz7mNxKrYzEtJuQ-4rWBkqCQlxKBJwLTxHVhyphenhyphenbuZ4oAIMcWPLpawplQHkETz6gD2PmDcwLLQea2mVSODfCTi6bO3icldLeWgOzrQwAvBkWPDZHYZUjIsKfDeZ-kpsbkok6dvC8XHjwzT1tByxTjj5-KOwLXDl_QBnlEp9aPvWw0yuBVLIo_zcySuyja4/s320/PXL_20260421_212552899.NIGHT.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjXz7mNxKrYzEtJuQ-4rWBkqCQlxKBJwLTxHVhyphenhyphenbuZ4oAIMcWPLpawplQHkETz6gD2PmDcwLLQea2mVSODfCTi6bO3icldLeWgOzrQwAvBkWPDZHYZUjIsKfDeZ-kpsbkok6dvC8XHjwzT1tByxTjj5-KOwLXDl_QBnlEp9aPvWw0yuBVLIo_zcySuyja4/s4080/PXL_20260421_212552899.NIGHT.jpg)
It boots great.
I think it's time for a proper tour of the system using some additional disk images from the ibilio archive, but before we do, I'm also a bit tired of taking fisheye pictures of the monitor screen. The signal sent to the VR201 monitor is actually just high quality monochrome composite video, at least in 80 column mode, and some DEC users without a monitor of their own have fashioned dongle connectors that split up the DA-15 in the back into video, power and keyboard lines. But we have an iffy monitor here that has all the necessary ports already. Let's just add a composite jack to it, and this modified monitor will also work for monochrome grabs from our DEC Pro 380 in the future (and maybe a Rainbow if I decide to hunt one down).
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi5Gi88yyGqY-VjWjqRCR7TS4LputAw7z-CE_7KCcMMi4MGayqnEcSO-fbeWvEBY69UgreY-YOL_r-hsuheobEXzGOU_K16XP8y9u_4szActuViU5uPG_Ge8kUurEHlb48ocJMF3Evu5z5n4yfCtuH1Lez6Tmlx_MrfbSYcfm1jaTjqMFv8td1x9w_C4Fo/s320/PXL_20260421_195559876.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi5Gi88yyGqY-VjWjqRCR7TS4LputAw7z-CE_7KCcMMi4MGayqnEcSO-fbeWvEBY69UgreY-YOL_r-hsuheobEXzGOU_K16XP8y9u_4szActuViU5uPG_Ge8kUurEHlb48ocJMF3Evu5z5n4yfCtuH1Lez6Tmlx_MrfbSYcfm1jaTjqMFv8td1x9w_C4Fo/s4080/PXL_20260421_195559876.jpg)
The VR201 is quite easy to disassemble. (The usual warning when working on vintage monitors: CRTs contain high voltage and can maintain a dangerous charge for some period after use. Do not attempt if you are uncomfortable or unskilled with working around potentially energized glass tubes of this sort.) First, to get it out of the way, on the underside of the monitor press in the spring-loaded height adjust slide and pull the height pole all the way out from the monitor.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhNZ5XCWNF2QzmlQM94heYIs-Dbox-6ufMbl_7d4uHyuycg4nHWVUGBJ_acU1X3yTjZrtwHEYlsarbSgm6iSs1SCBjqTaPUs_wnS4AkiPHJObj9eNNVP9b_Z-mZEFGa0BzrymS7hNgyCIzzBU66d-h69KK-Oo3BQJUy61Cn25zVpo_Tb9UCEw1jL4IktA0/s320/PXL_20260421_194957120~2.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhNZ5XCWNF2QzmlQM94heYIs-Dbox-6ufMbl_7d4uHyuycg4nHWVUGBJ_acU1X3yTjZrtwHEYlsarbSgm6iSs1SCBjqTaPUs_wnS4AkiPHJObj9eNNVP9b_Z-mZEFGa0BzrymS7hNgyCIzzBU66d-h69KK-Oo3BQJUy61Cn25zVpo_Tb9UCEw1jL4IktA0/s4080/PXL_20260421_194957120~2.jpg)
Next, with a spudger or carefully with a flathead screwdriver, pop this button cover off the back.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgTCJVWNpYjBHxixmAzQ8X5gGiDtuseGnYunDubSfCWQvH4MgCeTbN5VYFLuoAbT1WK8_r_TQ-MU7ICDefatb1EZzsDOD7gH6Da8ZbQ9uQrhDZtPOrjcmx2iKV4eZukytM7b3S46sAO0g_4YoVYBPVyb4TTgPqs1Jfg9o7VC9nfZQD309ZO6A6HUjTEXHU/s320/PXL_20260421_195126767~2.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgTCJVWNpYjBHxixmAzQ8X5gGiDtuseGnYunDubSfCWQvH4MgCeTbN5VYFLuoAbT1WK8_r_TQ-MU7ICDefatb1EZzsDOD7gH6Da8ZbQ9uQrhDZtPOrjcmx2iKV4eZukytM7b3S46sAO0g_4YoVYBPVyb4TTgPqs1Jfg9o7VC9nfZQD309ZO6A6HUjTEXHU/s4080/PXL_20260421_195126767~2.jpg)
Under the button cover is a single Phillips-head screw. Remove that and you can slide the entire rear plastic shell off.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhRy6Rgr9ys1RA5m6KOb6deWcNRtfPeQQJcOAWv8W9Z5s-jAYwsx9DTkpuXZP1Yeswbh4_8X1TtyZXa_9JT34CQvpBNKLNbIFRq0CyPTVZnd0_YIin0Sc3sbFR6Y0Tp-pKIQtBv8aqn9B9PV2vsXZPSEyZneK64rMKCE2uNicHcrlUdVn8Dku_fdDYDRZs/s320/PXL_20260421_200107808~2.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhRy6Rgr9ys1RA5m6KOb6deWcNRtfPeQQJcOAWv8W9Z5s-jAYwsx9DTkpuXZP1Yeswbh4_8X1TtyZXa_9JT34CQvpBNKLNbIFRq0CyPTVZnd0_YIin0Sc3sbFR6Y0Tp-pKIQtBv8aqn9B9PV2vsXZPSEyZneK64rMKCE2uNicHcrlUdVn8Dku_fdDYDRZs/s4080/PXL_20260421_200107808~2.jpg)
The circuit bar and CRT both sit in a metal cage. Here I have oriented the monitor so it's sitting on the circuit board side, but the circuit board is actually on _top_ of the monitor, so this view is looking at the underside. We don't need to disturb the CRT for what we're going to do, but if you want to [discharge the CRT for safety](https://www.ifixit.com/Wiki/Safely_discharging_CRT_monitors), this view shows where to find the suction cup anode.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgnZFUfSj-OfB0WoPy4nWGA4ddC8o4t8hd58RV7kP4AINI0F0c7Xn1y_bed8Jygjm26lxqTK4_Azyxa-qDnX8_JwhlYbtITDQnzPiK20dg6W0ndKWEXhf-U8EOfLz53MUlYEIWajoCF3ou5sVaQaWSN7wrt5JRvWRFPFEp4C7bGUobbgIl6GEubMotvtnI/s320/PXL_20260421_200152563.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgnZFUfSj-OfB0WoPy4nWGA4ddC8o4t8hd58RV7kP4AINI0F0c7Xn1y_bed8Jygjm26lxqTK4_Azyxa-qDnX8_JwhlYbtITDQnzPiK20dg6W0ndKWEXhf-U8EOfLz53MUlYEIWajoCF3ou5sVaQaWSN7wrt5JRvWRFPFEp4C7bGUobbgIl6GEubMotvtnI/s4080/PXL_20260421_200152563.jpg)
The usual CRT adjustment pots are behind the tube.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhjgsKiyu7h-9TDyoc9XTX2JeSWQ2s9HdDlb5ueOA1n4VZYCKFAT-z9duM849iEDdAEiiyeskoYuR4mU4MwQXIA-UwzL10oG_mdrp-p7wK4bvFMWJ2KsICDLHg21CgzRYfGeyURCGijNxZAqMkZw828Clj3P-MHPNK9ZjqXryi1_g1J8KoIXIJTuHCFCrk/s320/PXL_20260421_200431816.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhjgsKiyu7h-9TDyoc9XTX2JeSWQ2s9HdDlb5ueOA1n4VZYCKFAT-z9duM849iEDdAEiiyeskoYuR4mU4MwQXIA-UwzL10oG_mdrp-p7wK4bvFMWJ2KsICDLHg21CgzRYfGeyURCGijNxZAqMkZw828Clj3P-MHPNK9ZjqXryi1_g1J8KoIXIJTuHCFCrk/s4080/PXL_20260421_200431816.jpg)
What we'll do is solder a composite jack directly to the video input lines as a tap. This will necessarily bleed off some of the signal to the monitor when the jack is connected, but we don't really care about that for this purpose (if you did care, you'd probably want to pre-amplify the video signal a bit). We'll access the underside of the monitor's DA-15 by undoing these two top screws and folding back the Tyvek non-conductive shield.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEg4X-2utssBMjpzoR9NJF15OPOjRFvZDiJSI_e6W-EdHYGV2AYlEzDGVF8BB3J1hEH_H7V-hzEq6R-hpfP8Uh-kWK7TzOPe5pBFeKZCrVuRgsNK5fTSsfK5AOdfC6EntGX6I3pGCtdmwXdcJmG4vGNU-ei1FNGZm92CqtDalY32HX7y7G764vDgWpcTx8I/s320/PXL_20260421_200540619.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEg4X-2utssBMjpzoR9NJF15OPOjRFvZDiJSI_e6W-EdHYGV2AYlEzDGVF8BB3J1hEH_H7V-hzEq6R-hpfP8Uh-kWK7TzOPe5pBFeKZCrVuRgsNK5fTSsfK5AOdfC6EntGX6I3pGCtdmwXdcJmG4vGNU-ei1FNGZm92CqtDalY32HX7y7G764vDgWpcTx8I/s4080/PXL_20260421_200540619.jpg)
Using the [BCC02 cable pinout](https://www.larosse.net/pc100/dec100-vr201-cable.html), you can see multiple lines fused together into a single ground and two others into a single +12V, and the rest for keyboard, video and colour lines, which are irrelevant here. On this hand-routed PCB the power and ground conglomerations are obvious from their fatter traces. To avoid the risk of causing a short near the +12V power lines on the DA-15 connector itself, I decided to only attach ground there and looked for another solder point with continuity to pin 12, the monochrome video input. I found a nice strong signal on the "highlighted" junction up and to the right (northeast) of the rightmost/easternmost screw in this picture, about 4/5ths of the way to the right.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiDuKy3c4Zbg1otIIgwGzqC_L41AK425j50eoxNom8pTTbF1amQozqfgvUoTC2ayxku__e7Hlk0G4ueeC95ZgBKXmjB7goiWTYdkruKIP-GlnVAVUUEEwaDa5Gcxg0-NLe-YpPxiEUEbLUIwTB_2XsRYQ5_IYghnfQvFSn05Q7OSW92sSoWtLvnrkrevOU/s320/PXL_20260421_215717549.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiDuKy3c4Zbg1otIIgwGzqC_L41AK425j50eoxNom8pTTbF1amQozqfgvUoTC2ayxku__e7Hlk0G4ueeC95ZgBKXmjB7goiWTYdkruKIP-GlnVAVUUEEwaDa5Gcxg0-NLe-YpPxiEUEbLUIwTB_2XsRYQ5_IYghnfQvFSn05Q7OSW92sSoWtLvnrkrevOU/s4080/PXL_20260421_215717549.jpg)
I put the signal line on that point, then soldered the ground to the big ground junction on the port.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEh8CBYKqO3AgTx7G_4rfc2yczRZntTGVEpTG6B8UMYFp7Lg5raNLcZc-TjwRssd84Tt_eRgBPw9g6-PW5v3QOVX-Nxin5DpwuVzJMomUyG9ztZNc80mrWEvdofnruWNMkZmgzLOv07-NpB2xKZwCQNys626ABzQ8qkafs_CHze3AlvkNXjp2FZD5bJLSNI/s320/PXL_20260421_203708535.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEh8CBYKqO3AgTx7G_4rfc2yczRZntTGVEpTG6B8UMYFp7Lg5raNLcZc-TjwRssd84Tt_eRgBPw9g6-PW5v3QOVX-Nxin5DpwuVzJMomUyG9ztZNc80mrWEvdofnruWNMkZmgzLOv07-NpB2xKZwCQNys626ABzQ8qkafs_CHze3AlvkNXjp2FZD5bJLSNI/s4080/PXL_20260421_203708535.jpg)
With the cable attached, I grabbed another of the same size and took the plastic shell out to the workshop to cut a hole for it. We'll route it out through the ventilation slots.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgzIiF-sAAUS_9CE2z8qVnW4NZIwbOKoVdb5Jxfek4Sic8OWkIJtV3qzwsDFOqucSL5CJnS-ap4MnKoHF5oL2CO5f-8ksOMEAYBZi6RXKUR58n5JwSOFnDzIRvPzf9QV3rmG1QPbyrzz4GMQTERPErsHAFSWDbHM-41SuHFSGetVn8YHsuZOdUGL274HZg/s320/PXL_20260421_204139363~2.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgzIiF-sAAUS_9CE2z8qVnW4NZIwbOKoVdb5Jxfek4Sic8OWkIJtV3qzwsDFOqucSL5CJnS-ap4MnKoHF5oL2CO5f-8ksOMEAYBZi6RXKUR58n5JwSOFnDzIRvPzf9QV3rmG1QPbyrzz4GMQTERPErsHAFSWDbHM-41SuHFSGetVn8YHsuZOdUGL274HZg/s4080/PXL_20260421_204139363~2.jpg)
Buffing the hole with the Dremel to clean it up a little.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiNVcv2EJLFxelPE-03Ggm6VbbvsOtcSe1i0C-JqnTfbITgAAOheH3wo4Und2uycI0swP1s1rcCoGl1CnDNMIWmO_8ML9dOFS-IMa7g5mkIBx23gPF2rgpm3A_6bxUE5Ao0OY0pSMbGu281KoVAZ9Vogstzg22KLe207oPkHR80-ClkOwQV3RiJlDzKC9Y/s320/PXL_20260421_225547015.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiNVcv2EJLFxelPE-03Ggm6VbbvsOtcSe1i0C-JqnTfbITgAAOheH3wo4Und2uycI0swP1s1rcCoGl1CnDNMIWmO_8ML9dOFS-IMa7g5mkIBx23gPF2rgpm3A_6bxUE5Ao0OY0pSMbGu281KoVAZ9Vogstzg22KLe207oPkHR80-ClkOwQV3RiJlDzKC9Y/s4080/PXL_20260421_225547015.jpg)
To accommodate the new cable I removed the rear plate over the ports and thumbwheels, then routed the cable down, around and out the hole.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEghCcqY-HQevI8y9afoOJyPu1r8br7vrLyZ_9XU1my_O2SX_twr7UepRnk31KcBHwZ86Cby30eQ0uZ4ecPXlu79ZPL1_OIIokqZ_Oi5rsB2y_inYOHkm5-AUq-So1iYS-phThU3O4gqvawuNun1A7w8h7aI2WFbPsJn3AGlnuD30vM3H2V7TMr-HF5O2_Y/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h00m12s573.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEghCcqY-HQevI8y9afoOJyPu1r8br7vrLyZ_9XU1my_O2SX_twr7UepRnk31KcBHwZ86Cby30eQ0uZ4ecPXlu79ZPL1_OIIokqZ_Oi5rsB2y_inYOHkm5-AUq-So1iYS-phThU3O4gqvawuNun1A7w8h7aI2WFbPsJn3AGlnuD30vM3H2V7TMr-HF5O2_Y/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h00m12s573.png.jpg)
These captures were done directly from our hacked-in composite output. Although it cuts off the rightmost column and a rasterline or two at the bottom, which might indicate the signal isn't _quite_ standard, this isn't noticeable most of the time and the image quality is very good. However, I forgot to plug the keyboard in before I turned it on (so error 16). We'll fix that and then drive around the block.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiD_gLZ-rF4HMQEaLLS3zLJh-0jIuy4LEwn6qSkuKugkdBa12TxX1MLZ-witUOqyZr-AI_vxGG9GHKuiuZD7YyLhK0YoG-Lfal8J4uSCOFjmTXKI3HX3CEBMETf9xmKx4AWUzh9AOWANM2O8HtN37rfRb-qxbmCzFsJmNHXULVPaZ-vbJEHXZvP_OCGddI/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-19h59m43s415.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiD_gLZ-rF4HMQEaLLS3zLJh-0jIuy4LEwn6qSkuKugkdBa12TxX1MLZ-witUOqyZr-AI_vxGG9GHKuiuZD7YyLhK0YoG-Lfal8J4uSCOFjmTXKI3HX3CEBMETf9xmKx4AWUzh9AOWANM2O8HtN37rfRb-qxbmCzFsJmNHXULVPaZ-vbJEHXZvP_OCGddI/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-19h59m43s415.png.jpg)
Ready to start. One more check of the System Test.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiWllI5t9hPLA3S0kxwA28WPEMSSNrAs11DHCg5T22KT493_0TsTNNG5nQN56ooLY11ZyF59NK6CQzslbZGVnWnrevgDf0kUtp2EU4KPomtDwaICZbo6tz4hVahg-sabRGzMjA006JKBiJb3OnezgPHKWtSzwKp9MJPotD1kmHGd3nE16cxfwaq1ctAyaw/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h05m52s297.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiWllI5t9hPLA3S0kxwA28WPEMSSNrAs11DHCg5T22KT493_0TsTNNG5nQN56ooLY11ZyF59NK6CQzslbZGVnWnrevgDf0kUtp2EU4KPomtDwaICZbo6tz4hVahg-sabRGzMjA006JKBiJb3OnezgPHKWtSzwKp9MJPotD1kmHGd3nE16cxfwaq1ctAyaw/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h05m52s297.png.jpg)
And it's good.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjJJ7sCpxlNZShLV3Gna7hhKjgVaC8q0k-0LY7Fv7qcaMBKxh9ybuIlu_ZYJOessJWO0-t3mNQxUmT2CeLgBQ2xr0ABZbH96HPb-o1CoImGEF_xsQXH2YmCtq3usjjoEFGTuBFt91O9eI2fepIjvvr5zu6JxaBAeCW08zSgJkG7tuuOS2KEXffwKVMa3Bw/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h06m26s310.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjJJ7sCpxlNZShLV3Gna7hhKjgVaC8q0k-0LY7Fv7qcaMBKxh9ybuIlu_ZYJOessJWO0-t3mNQxUmT2CeLgBQ2xr0ABZbH96HPb-o1CoImGEF_xsQXH2YmCtq3usjjoEFGTuBFt91O9eI2fepIjvvr5zu6JxaBAeCW08zSgJkG7tuuOS2KEXffwKVMa3Bw/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h06m26s310.png.jpg)
Back into the startup menu to start our first package. We'll spend a little time with WPS-8, which is nearly the whole reason the DECmate series existed. The version we'll use here is a 1987 release from the DECmate III era to give you the full breadth of what was possible.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiBZQugwbvDwq0KPpMKzhK6gTQkzgmnfM4JPJPQYFd1D0g4zVyHuQKpN4LzfyaRePcAQe9HgW-aB-1Q-fYoyT7D_i_Jfd6hVlA8vMjbwEe9x4RnoVAWcEExAPYearESPbhnL56oDegZVfsihgV3qnOWPziR8z_yuG1dAylqC3CMrkjatIm-kOBnOqRRo0M/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h13m36s906.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiBZQugwbvDwq0KPpMKzhK6gTQkzgmnfM4JPJPQYFd1D0g4zVyHuQKpN4LzfyaRePcAQe9HgW-aB-1Q-fYoyT7D_i_Jfd6hVlA8vMjbwEe9x4RnoVAWcEExAPYearESPbhnL56oDegZVfsihgV3qnOWPziR8z_yuG1dAylqC3CMrkjatIm-kOBnOqRRo0M/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h13m36s906.png.jpg)
DEC's Word Processing System was a comprehensive solution, evolving from a basic terminal-based word processor on the VT78 into (by this period) a remote-enabled, multiuser document management package. WPS-8 is the PDP-8 standalone version which scaled from single user to concurrent terminals in the WPS-8/MTS variant; it was the first iteration of the WPS family, developed by a team at Digital (including Dan Bricklin, later to co-create VisiCalc) for the DEC Datasystem 310W based on the 8/A, and then quickly ported to the VT78/WS78 and DECmates. Although dedicated word processing systems from companies like NBI and Lexitron were already on the market, WPS-8 was the first such package written for a general purpose computer. A parallel system called WPS-11M ran as a task under RSX-11M on PDP-11 servers for document storage and tracking, and WPS-11M could send code to VT78/WS78 systems with specific ROMs so they could edit and upload documents from the local machine. This functionality was moved into software for the DECmates.
Regardless of how good it was at creating the office of the future, however, it isn't so good at accepting the _dates_ of the future. We'll just fudge that. Date and time are shown in the top right corner nearly everywhere except, oddly, when editing a document.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjFFsxZakQvQh6FbiANiG-n3VtPsRGWprIEWZjoOPslgHZSxWaMMzyE5-zDncLdEiQji_I8eLKUnaEObSld7yAfaNl1HQodiD-C3RiikKpSpvL2Crb_P85xaNbM8xSHc8DrpAHD-wkhiQ6Si6qUY2j7_lMJFAVxyLZ5tvkaWqIxd6rfJ-x1RMvzdoGS058/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h14m50s850.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjFFsxZakQvQh6FbiANiG-n3VtPsRGWprIEWZjoOPslgHZSxWaMMzyE5-zDncLdEiQji_I8eLKUnaEObSld7yAfaNl1HQodiD-C3RiikKpSpvL2Crb_P85xaNbM8xSHc8DrpAHD-wkhiQ6Si6qUY2j7_lMJFAVxyLZ5tvkaWqIxd6rfJ-x1RMvzdoGS058/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h14m50s850.png.jpg)
The menu system uses short one or two letter commands and sprawls over multiple pages. It starts with the basics, create, print and filesystem. Notice the default drive: on a dual-drive system it prefers to keep the system disk in one drive and your data disk in the other ("drive 1"). We'll set that up in a moment.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhkTKyoSrn_kygtqjrvgDWBUYxdU4RYhkH6OmGmIdE-degawySse3gF-ZiY9LWu1de1klyZj2qdNmYS9hC-v-_bh8IaMqCw7QK27iywfigdWPCKCu6M-JZOMOtMEoYW_h7NcVf_o5ZxRiJD68aUWLrLL59CjvesGf525KsLrntt-8u1LHFdBu99qGJgNnA/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h19m26s993.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhkTKyoSrn_kygtqjrvgDWBUYxdU4RYhkH6OmGmIdE-degawySse3gF-ZiY9LWu1de1klyZj2qdNmYS9hC-v-_bh8IaMqCw7QK27iywfigdWPCKCu6M-JZOMOtMEoYW_h7NcVf_o5ZxRiJD68aUWLrLL59CjvesGf525KsLrntt-8u1LHFdBu99qGJgNnA/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h19m26s993.png.jpg)
Additional optional packages included list processing support, which is basically DEC's take on form letters and mail merge, and a companion sort package that can rearrange list documents by field.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhOfCzAfXjjT3wIbX3htRriabSyNmsB8b0Mk_GWDgEg8ZSLE0CEp6VMSGzyyM0-bcEipI9_jszu8bsfxmaaA9iECNFzht_uRScrDTwC6DldagIQ5c6QLOP66AKiZXWsmXamhb2XDUszCYQw3dSUO9I4yrOyHnz7Tfk4OxCY3KV6EfNbrhDDPYdIgghYF7Y/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h21m35s199.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhOfCzAfXjjT3wIbX3htRriabSyNmsB8b0Mk_GWDgEg8ZSLE0CEp6VMSGzyyM0-bcEipI9_jszu8bsfxmaaA9iECNFzht_uRScrDTwC6DldagIQ5c6QLOP66AKiZXWsmXamhb2XDUszCYQw3dSUO9I4yrOyHnz7Tfk4OxCY3KV6EfNbrhDDPYdIgghYF7Y/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h21m35s199.png.jpg)
The character transmission package replaced the VT78's WPS-11M downloadable client functionality, becoming regular software loaded from floppy into the DECmate. It could use a directly attached serial port or a modem connection to the remote PDP-11 to send and access documents.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEivaUODcYqLK5ahS3-Vx1B3ap_AJNfTf9ZeB_OEvgFYFcYlYlntlX0_A2Wfd9iFr39k4XQa8dch8riEJXeWC6ocqJ0my0XqEIVFj38mPsghA2JwRtw_JaT5efRt0M52_AhVIusdzDKcAJeJNPNO6NGKLnzeuezJGISYmbL0OEZVB3XR78Zg6Yw4prc-ciw/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h22m10s012.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEivaUODcYqLK5ahS3-Vx1B3ap_AJNfTf9ZeB_OEvgFYFcYlYlntlX0_A2Wfd9iFr39k4XQa8dch8riEJXeWC6ocqJ0my0XqEIVFj38mPsghA2JwRtw_JaT5efRt0M52_AhVIusdzDKcAJeJNPNO6NGKLnzeuezJGISYmbL0OEZVB3XR78Zg6Yw4prc-ciw/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h22m10s012.png.jpg)
Disk management tools to backup floppies and system diskettes were also provided. There was no copy protection on the system diskette and DEC even encouraged you to make copies of it. You could store documents on the system disk as well, which was useful on a single drive system, but is rather less necessary with an RX50. However, the disk initialization options here only erase the disk to set up the proper storage structures for WPS-8: they don't low-level format it, and DEC was careful to call out the difference in the documentation. Only 8" disks could be formatted by WPS-8. For the RX50, you still had to buy your pre-formatted 5.25" floppies from DEC at mildly confiscatory rates, or use your appropriately configured Rainbow, or find one of the unofficial "Clandestine Disk Formatters" that could do so on the hardware itself. You then initialized that pre-formatted floppy here.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhoPeK82bQKcsrC24bZkXUn_bqTUIMKuX6WIT-1olQKFXBQ-0kHXVntQgXRYM8_MDl5TbZ2rBI7RyRcc4wfKWbdkFex-oDstqq554WUw2cPHqrLHphZ_RbRE5z_303YsCZX3ZU7KBjnl1ZH3Ut07Ri6g0oCLv5FYcsNnFd7DAg9Jvp58y3wgZBNSc19Z9o/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h22m36s279.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhoPeK82bQKcsrC24bZkXUn_bqTUIMKuX6WIT-1olQKFXBQ-0kHXVntQgXRYM8_MDl5TbZ2rBI7RyRcc4wfKWbdkFex-oDstqq554WUw2cPHqrLHphZ_RbRE5z_303YsCZX3ZU7KBjnl1ZH3Ut07Ri6g0oCLv5FYcsNnFd7DAg9Jvp58y3wgZBNSc19Z9o/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h22m36s279.png.jpg)
As it turns out, we don't have that problem anyway because we have a clean blank RX50 image loaded in the second drive. This will become the lower Gotek's disk image #0.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhDj9AIX85pgcDJga-1u5XsjICvOypktOvrK57mkyom4nZJNv8OBZ56iMUuAFaGhGiVipxwvBMFC2BrpKNEti1NRKci_sC4JhbhncTWPHTKcIAGJZXpx4fXSDD1c0YDCh-5ATLGfPZqx-H9qYI8auFd8EFIjpENzM38KQOW3uouSUk-f0oOopuxBsZKPS8/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h24m15s135.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhDj9AIX85pgcDJga-1u5XsjICvOypktOvrK57mkyom4nZJNv8OBZ56iMUuAFaGhGiVipxwvBMFC2BrpKNEti1NRKci_sC4JhbhncTWPHTKcIAGJZXpx4fXSDD1c0YDCh-5ATLGfPZqx-H9qYI8auFd8EFIjpENzM38KQOW3uouSUk-f0oOopuxBsZKPS8/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h24m15s135.png.jpg)
The process is not fast, though the Gotek greatly speeds it up; while the manual says to expect about three minutes to complete the process, we readied our virtual WPS-8 document disk from start to finish in just over 90 seconds. The Gold MENU key referenced here is PF1 followed by M on the LK401, but you can also just press F9. On the official DECmate II keyboard the Gold key was indeed indicated by a gold plastic keycap that was otherwise blank.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjq2s8UxGeZSSqO8-sjSweZhDOnlqwx7tZ6BKZzHllyaEy1L93bZvaC34omR9Pfh2CwR_Zam5Rg4lvqYNfsbBc5_fXi2a6zzYYgXjeP-wetZvCFLJSszS2JKt1nI3wKJ7q6UOQndxN2OyQ4j3FKLLM2tbc8ddsYg2zmq7L77Jr472oNyFmjrX-JBMTI_2E/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h25m16s001.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjq2s8UxGeZSSqO8-sjSweZhDOnlqwx7tZ6BKZzHllyaEy1L93bZvaC34omR9Pfh2CwR_Zam5Rg4lvqYNfsbBc5_fXi2a6zzYYgXjeP-wetZvCFLJSszS2JKt1nI3wKJ7q6UOQndxN2OyQ4j3FKLLM2tbc8ddsYg2zmq7L77Jr472oNyFmjrX-JBMTI_2E/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h25m16s001.png.jpg)
Some of the other WPS-8 menu shortcuts.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhStMe6u3bi7Jw5THrHp48DNwZ9hIxt68INk6_tmmwgZx1PjXseLSiVW-5RqlStu4i3guEJ3TXeWVxozly0QfLwKIjPpThB9nMqqpiimgauixC5gpmxfNq5jlKtzkHOIXTB2esWZLO_TF5OS5pLntsGzqJCeLfsm3zoxkd2IA08OPIXPgpBOe9ZI8D6MiQ/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h25m29s014.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhStMe6u3bi7Jw5THrHp48DNwZ9hIxt68INk6_tmmwgZx1PjXseLSiVW-5RqlStu4i3guEJ3TXeWVxozly0QfLwKIjPpThB9nMqqpiimgauixC5gpmxfNq5jlKtzkHOIXTB2esWZLO_TF5OS5pLntsGzqJCeLfsm3zoxkd2IA08OPIXPgpBOe9ZI8D6MiQ/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h25m29s014.png.jpg)
Let's create a simple document of our own by using the C option.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhUupYBLGxzLOZcQ1QiT6ScD06WM0hgiYtbiG1epjdwj0IF0ZVdLPiXQi0mL2lWqmrisJ60WYrctJtAsrz3RExxo5BbAeWpNAbIm60XTmN9Tqb0aJ3J9YGeXXUgXsOsp5MHAQyfJYyEt8v3BfrczXAN8ple21VXtVtexttyQ3G9H2m0gnAgcEiZEp9PpTk/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h25m44s966.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhUupYBLGxzLOZcQ1QiT6ScD06WM0hgiYtbiG1epjdwj0IF0ZVdLPiXQi0mL2lWqmrisJ60WYrctJtAsrz3RExxo5BbAeWpNAbIm60XTmN9Tqb0aJ3J9YGeXXUgXsOsp5MHAQyfJYyEt8v3BfrczXAN8ple21VXtVtexttyQ3G9H2m0gnAgcEiZEp9PpTk/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h25m44s966.png.jpg)
One notable thing about WPS-8, especially for the era, is that its document naming rules were very free given it was intended for relatively non-technical users. Document names could be up to 71 characters long, in mixed case, permitting spaces, numbers and most symbols except for angle brackets. The only notable restrictions were that a filename could not start with {, \[, or a number, or start or end with spaces (though all of those characters were legal otherwise), and you shouldn't name a file something that completely contains the name of another file (like `Monthly` vs `Monthly newsletter`) or you might make the file inaccessible by that name. We'll talk about why these restrictions exist when we get to the disk index. Here we will write a short and pointed letter to Kenneth Olsen.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEh8LB2JX-4eEIBzCX0_YU1cuyCntFNEsp9WmKqKXJ4ipSeVqTADSsklGYen5knR3Fg-fftrlSwJpKr7XxObSMM2zEC7Mjek0hG6kN8PGHlfzjFPDa9Q_02cSy-0QXzMGeGj0ClesPgeY9HCVgloMb06PBMnxBZHK25gq6qZkDtMvWWCUcGD97C80ZoGswo/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h28m11s078.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEh8LB2JX-4eEIBzCX0_YU1cuyCntFNEsp9WmKqKXJ4ipSeVqTADSsklGYen5knR3Fg-fftrlSwJpKr7XxObSMM2zEC7Mjek0hG6kN8PGHlfzjFPDa9Q_02cSy-0QXzMGeGj0ClesPgeY9HCVgloMb06PBMnxBZHK25gq6qZkDtMvWWCUcGD97C80ZoGswo/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h28m11s078.png.jpg)
Intended for non-proportional dot matrix and daisywheel printers, the display is as WYSIWYG as you'll get on a terminal, with a small "ruler" indicating left and right character margins. Word wrap respects these margins, and adjusting them would automatically reflow the document accordingly, repaginating it as necessary (widow and orphan adjustment could be optionally enabled). This ruler could also be used to mark tab stops and justification points for more advanced formatting, and could be saved to the system disk for regular reuse. A top status bar tracks your cursor's page, line, and character position. The DECmates could show bold and underlined text. Combinations with the Gold key are used to mark text, to set attributes, to move by word, sentence or paragraph, or to cut and paste.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhUpOWAdc-Zv414w12K2FaiEcUS0vVKg_4ZaAg0PVx9NkkU8J6IIbEl7izRbUrq9zwagS_buirbLNdH6dp-j8BCiQ8jw3q0DpbrXUKA6DnN4SkzBCwD45dH8SMamE-ZbiN-NkXMmFiJsD9an1i72-7hTVOH03zmtjZVNuDunUFj0rKvnTaJffZWE8uwwiw/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h28m30s986.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhUpOWAdc-Zv414w12K2FaiEcUS0vVKg_4ZaAg0PVx9NkkU8J6IIbEl7izRbUrq9zwagS_buirbLNdH6dp-j8BCiQ8jw3q0DpbrXUKA6DnN4SkzBCwD45dH8SMamE-ZbiN-NkXMmFiJsD9an1i72-7hTVOH03zmtjZVNuDunUFj0rKvnTaJffZWE8uwwiw/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h28m30s986.png.jpg)
When you were done, Gold MENU would bring you to this options screen. The math option was for the optional Math package, allowing live calculations in the document, sort of a proto-spreadsheet not unlike the [Canon Cat](/2024/07/pretty-pictures-bootable-floppy-disks.html). You could also adjust the page layout, print, do search and replace, reference a library document for prewritten text, or define "user keys" for storing a sequence of keys as a macro. Library documents and user keys, along with text shortcut abbreviations, could also be saved on a system disk.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjb-685L0F4GcSPIDzPCyvVhOq8uJIa_Bd3CVx-7CWgMwaiUilfMEz3_5oytkv0Zb7u4P34gYyMcGbcvRrZehgir9IL1-t3KjOK_oOZk0Ac3Xu96yvCkVTzrt-T4fAjaLPnjPoQ4Jdf4N-HI8XM5WS_eTnNM2LIwohDCW8J-TkaNlRzzt1dH974b7JnR6E/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h28m57s313.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjb-685L0F4GcSPIDzPCyvVhOq8uJIa_Bd3CVx-7CWgMwaiUilfMEz3_5oytkv0Zb7u4P34gYyMcGbcvRrZehgir9IL1-t3KjOK_oOZk0Ac3Xu96yvCkVTzrt-T4fAjaLPnjPoQ4Jdf4N-HI8XM5WS_eTnNM2LIwohDCW8J-TkaNlRzzt1dH974b7JnR6E/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h28m57s313.png.jpg)
We'll pick the F option to file the document, which will save it to our virtual data disk in the lower Gotek.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhErvh19I-XMyUkbEuNdDo4sO6WH3V6S7xEStIMjEUuLrDincKI28SDB6efrVo-7klYz_AdGib-u8jRkrkA52G5gAU-gsDTKAH-gYJOo2sR-tvNa7MGwKsyDgtIFh6n0tQ9_WKb57finOMJL2CXK9bNNgAL10cOPwNCoo89Yi7tcuhhlY17Q4hwTPpXy7c/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h29m38s043.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhErvh19I-XMyUkbEuNdDo4sO6WH3V6S7xEStIMjEUuLrDincKI28SDB6efrVo-7klYz_AdGib-u8jRkrkA52G5gAU-gsDTKAH-gYJOo2sR-tvNa7MGwKsyDgtIFh6n0tQ9_WKb57finOMJL2CXK9bNNgAL10cOPwNCoo89Yi7tcuhhlY17Q4hwTPpXy7c/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h29m38s043.png.jpg)
The disk directory can be seen from the menu's "index" option. (Again, apologies for the last column being cut off.) This is stored as a phantom document on the disk called the `<Index Document>`. Each document has a number and the index document is always document 1, created as part of the initialization process and automatically maintained by the system as documents are created or deleted. It contains a mapping of filenames to document numbers for loading a file by name, though you can also use a document number directly, which is why you can't start a filename with a number. When retrieving a file by name the system searches for the first match in the file, which will accept partial matches, so if you had file `Monthly newsletter` as document 2 and `Monthly` as document 3, loading `Monthly` would actually retrieve document 2 (which is why you shouldn't name files substrings of other filenames).
You can edit the index document like any other document and this is in fact the easiest way to do things like renaming, but you'll notice that there are angle brackets in that file to indicate the file number (which is why you can't use angle brackets in a filename, and they are also used as metacharacters in things like abbreviation definitions). It is possible and supported to create an index document that refers to documents scattered across multiple disks (a "master index").
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhk3UTC1La6Bg5rC0133QrGtfdzV6zGZH-lRocXyuA2rZmteIBnwvoucpUDVaKDsGV3IOpoxXFlaRyQqMqEpbKBpoY2DxWTYsbDajksXu6uCXJLTg1V8zLTH3PdzGZWZ5nPMZ-fydjIjoxL2sKqHCHLJYY88PmgrqmfpuLQhlQMign_wEBBX6UYx327tug/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h32m35s284.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhk3UTC1La6Bg5rC0133QrGtfdzV6zGZH-lRocXyuA2rZmteIBnwvoucpUDVaKDsGV3IOpoxXFlaRyQqMqEpbKBpoY2DxWTYsbDajksXu6uCXJLTg1V8zLTH3PdzGZWZ5nPMZ-fydjIjoxL2sKqHCHLJYY88PmgrqmfpuLQhlQMign_wEBBX6UYx327tug/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h32m35s284.png.jpg)
Finally, the program prefers you to explicitly finish before you power off or leave the system. This not only ensures everything is written out and files are closed, but also clears the paste buffer (in case you're just leaving for lunch and don't want someone snarfing your deleted text).
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhG-kxPrdck3PSZRQ-kPW_iLVfSogog4xEalqI6j6QpiZkveOO5D5dyw1RJs5aY_HO5uLxL-EPnBI0TscYcojLELLgwciQ8QT_ix_3dS18VujXMgUMJ9sLvjulmeVmXWLyd-r6hKgQjf7iDFzhFiPqSvNeHH99bET_fl4dwE6Dv_bFwL7e5976mdMdXiW4/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h41m21s152.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhG-kxPrdck3PSZRQ-kPW_iLVfSogog4xEalqI6j6QpiZkveOO5D5dyw1RJs5aY_HO5uLxL-EPnBI0TscYcojLELLgwciQ8QT_ix_3dS18VujXMgUMJ9sLvjulmeVmXWLyd-r6hKgQjf7iDFzhFiPqSvNeHH99bET_fl4dwE6Dv_bFwL7e5976mdMdXiW4/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h41m21s152.png.jpg)
If WPS-8 was too big (or expensive) for you, DEC later offered a cut-down glass typewriter program called TYPEasy as a poor substitute.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjA4wKRzcWt1SvE_mgYWsFiskad9gFzCuFdqVxc_YLDK3C0GsPNmNR3qZKEC-kz2Bq0Vl9mWZ0pRmFiGkg8BinK2KxMRSmpVMu_QnDmNOcxz9BP2QRMNBaQ2ARTuxiA2jrVX75KGIb7YBJW7BI7OvRDKJTtMOqHIBbbnZHiPsB-HH4Zg9lSHRL_wFRScGQ/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h41m48s662.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjA4wKRzcWt1SvE_mgYWsFiskad9gFzCuFdqVxc_YLDK3C0GsPNmNR3qZKEC-kz2Bq0Vl9mWZ0pRmFiGkg8BinK2KxMRSmpVMu_QnDmNOcxz9BP2QRMNBaQ2ARTuxiA2jrVX75KGIb7YBJW7BI7OvRDKJTtMOqHIBbbnZHiPsB-HH4Zg9lSHRL_wFRScGQ/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h41m48s662.png.jpg)
TYPEasy has substantially fewer options and is primarily oriented to spewing text you type, either directly or (more helpfully) line by line, straight to an attached line printer. This is even less functional than the typewriter motif in Commodore's [Magic Desk](/2025/06/see-jane-128-by-arktronics-run.html), which at least could keep lines in memory.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEil75qEt08nclNHRs0aepp17WoO2jqg3FlW8HvIQJDpu68vCkidlJrkqvIx0r_c08yoR21CUNm4kNWwi_yIiy9aeBpz5enbE9GLqFi-Eg7zeqG-3uZ20Bn_Q2hIFPeeM72rMD-elvWqQ6t-7BgmU35NRwJSZsG_I_mR6S-XKc6NZ6xNh5NABXAwnOcCaOM/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h42m03s102.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEil75qEt08nclNHRs0aepp17WoO2jqg3FlW8HvIQJDpu68vCkidlJrkqvIx0r_c08yoR21CUNm4kNWwi_yIiy9aeBpz5enbE9GLqFi-Eg7zeqG-3uZ20Bn_Q2hIFPeeM72rMD-elvWqQ6t-7BgmU35NRwJSZsG_I_mR6S-XKc6NZ6xNh5NABXAwnOcCaOM/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h42m03s102.png.jpg)
In fact, you really can't do anything useful with it at all if you don't have a printer connected, and we don't. TYPEasy doesn't seem to have been very popular and there's little out there about it.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgsbHiXGu59lY5wx87fmClVhpMMupfgPGjHy8HYgsrDTykNE6IaY6sgl7Bodwh6KF-k1L7TU1sPRWt-TgyI1fY0vwcXGo880oc0fYdmrI4pzJtIjt64_zEYpfuGtf8aahwubBQDLkG24ttYKLWEx8S633ZZ5Hg8Vom8UGMRXJxDhyphenhyphenJX-Tehr-KhuYRujyk/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h33m26s680.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgsbHiXGu59lY5wx87fmClVhpMMupfgPGjHy8HYgsrDTykNE6IaY6sgl7Bodwh6KF-k1L7TU1sPRWt-TgyI1fY0vwcXGo880oc0fYdmrI4pzJtIjt64_zEYpfuGtf8aahwubBQDLkG24ttYKLWEx8S633ZZ5Hg8Vom8UGMRXJxDhyphenhyphenJX-Tehr-KhuYRujyk/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h33m26s680.png.jpg)
So, now that we have a working copy of it on the Gotek, we should revisit the System Overview next. This is actually a very extensive program and we'll just show a little bit for flavour. There were other interactive computer-based instruction disks for the DECmate, including ones specifically for WPS-8 and its optional packages.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEj70Y4JXXnLhGWadYzBWrHZaUdf1vWPGD2O86yBZhlZoSush61ZG0-jY3OxuLkFI7iB8TT6s0hzR07ufKOxzxiHmIlt2Tiwpl0FZPXfgV_ip0Sfxh5VisOC0qaLzQdSQwC9JQM6lTXSYSWi78yFlQuIUPTjy1Jl2C9QWwX4UX80jE1fW9YGOUbGKX0fchY/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h35m06s300.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEj70Y4JXXnLhGWadYzBWrHZaUdf1vWPGD2O86yBZhlZoSush61ZG0-jY3OxuLkFI7iB8TT6s0hzR07ufKOxzxiHmIlt2Tiwpl0FZPXfgV_ip0Sfxh5VisOC0qaLzQdSQwC9JQM6lTXSYSWi78yFlQuIUPTjy1Jl2C9QWwX4UX80jE1fW9YGOUbGKX0fchY/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h35m06s300.png.jpg)
The Overview and other CBI titles had detailed, stepwise instructions to a degree that might seem excessive to us nerds but was probably appreciated by office users at the time, and makes regular use of the keyboard's special keys.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi2irCiKN0SZ1VvLjdXlUbT2OdBQit5TDy2yRGMEp2HQKLGOEap25ox-Tmxds4HRzg1MTTbNumqUL0tqDDcOufmLfja2Nwm8bUOsBpG1sZb2O7wZoEL2Gum29d0NQZeOa-PpVCH3X4zEtipoUKnSsymXHz9WRg4DLVZXo2fNMVzSYpeoer3JD7PGWUNG4Q/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h35m27s124.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi2irCiKN0SZ1VvLjdXlUbT2OdBQit5TDy2yRGMEp2HQKLGOEap25ox-Tmxds4HRzg1MTTbNumqUL0tqDDcOufmLfja2Nwm8bUOsBpG1sZb2O7wZoEL2Gum29d0NQZeOa-PpVCH3X4zEtipoUKnSsymXHz9WRg4DLVZXo2fNMVzSYpeoer3JD7PGWUNG4Q/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h35m27s124.png.jpg)
Main menu. It is mercifully possible for advanced or partially trained users to skip ahead to an arbitrary topic.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhspyDXLAfDYQmo22uvg88wy0tBMpcEM3Lqey5nnjrItTV_IMVjZqCL-U3aSarhI1XeBwrLCxfT0gdALFQFq5BpC2MxqLN-cNykUEhkQV-oyNG2-E10qm7Hty5KKybDs9OTmg1oduWbUCAx-dgnvUWBxovy_c_wzNf2iZ2agnGjk62kdJ6Q1VbgmERz05Q/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h36m05s528.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhspyDXLAfDYQmo22uvg88wy0tBMpcEM3Lqey5nnjrItTV_IMVjZqCL-U3aSarhI1XeBwrLCxfT0gdALFQFq5BpC2MxqLN-cNykUEhkQV-oyNG2-E10qm7Hty5KKybDs9OTmg1oduWbUCAx-dgnvUWBxovy_c_wzNf2iZ2agnGjk62kdJ6Q1VbgmERz05Q/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h36m05s528.png.jpg)
However, some of what this disk has (and this is the disk we have, specifically for the DECmate II) seems to be inaccurate. This is legitimately where the Set-up key is on the official DECmate II keyboard (i.e., F3), but programs will not generally let you enter it on demand.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjBfjhKNdq1TwuZmksW_A7DKAJOVAK3O4KJSS1cIOtEsO3skszxQbHF6CTAr4uP8VWBHTNKyGkwlRBZFfk2z5PBO9h6PxRWxi3zRvYSwP8CWuhj7Bdy6f6QrVsSW-v8x76y2VMuALyrzAuGD7nuzwqbz2lkwlguOi3d3AO6Ie20oslXafhq7UpfZ3DOai8/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h37m11s726.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjBfjhKNdq1TwuZmksW_A7DKAJOVAK3O4KJSS1cIOtEsO3skszxQbHF6CTAr4uP8VWBHTNKyGkwlRBZFfk2z5PBO9h6PxRWxi3zRvYSwP8CWuhj7Bdy6f6QrVsSW-v8x76y2VMuALyrzAuGD7nuzwqbz2lkwlguOi3d3AO6Ie20oslXafhq7UpfZ3DOai8/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h37m11s726.png.jpg)
It nevertheless presents a reasonably accurate facsimile of the user experience, including the slushware setup screen, which we just "inverted." If you press the wrong key, the program will not let you continue until you press the correct one.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjJ10B726UZVcm7UqG9WN1kB84HG1ZYWKgxTCdvyr_3mlihsliXJJfYD07RhI2Gihv2nTuTO22H4tyfH7-lKROWeEBTETZaITVNYH08Vn6mhsoQZHH0I1H_CyZa-KnkFxqybz242XCW-fAOzp2nMiiKBtr4LM_OJzSYqnksJmKj6fnDqZ1s802KpDFsMW0/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h37m48s506.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjJ10B726UZVcm7UqG9WN1kB84HG1ZYWKgxTCdvyr_3mlihsliXJJfYD07RhI2Gihv2nTuTO22H4tyfH7-lKROWeEBTETZaITVNYH08Vn6mhsoQZHH0I1H_CyZa-KnkFxqybz242XCW-fAOzp2nMiiKBtr4LM_OJzSYqnksJmKj6fnDqZ1s802KpDFsMW0/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h37m48s506.png.jpg)
Units have a summary at the end for reinforcement. A unit number in the upper right corner allows you to monitor your progress through the lessons.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiiFa5o18YgrdPLhVIX_U5BiZ47tLrFztW6waz355M2WSezaKmkobCJUrc5lyiwH3q8krMEuYZaZIHcn42fqJrCzn4yhjNexqKS63YEq4mFp5hPyrcqUiqVmERzDAMo_zF3Zu5Fy2n9iM745ArkdsBld4-Z4IkY0_m8ngTmBwnY1-kvlCkoaBDxUN6ksiI/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h38m31s474.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEiiFa5o18YgrdPLhVIX_U5BiZ47tLrFztW6waz355M2WSezaKmkobCJUrc5lyiwH3q8krMEuYZaZIHcn42fqJrCzn4yhjNexqKS63YEq4mFp5hPyrcqUiqVmERzDAMo_zF3Zu5Fy2n9iM745ArkdsBld4-Z4IkY0_m8ngTmBwnY1-kvlCkoaBDxUN6ksiI/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h38m31s474.png.jpg)
Simple graphics and animation turn up in various places, such as this rendering of a 5.25" floppy.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEheVxhTFqUGUUU6tn92DiJjoLvWaX8dkoTV1FMgk6vNqwkHmcjqmwLt1FcJQrC7ux0RQB31mB3pyGfA-jhUxDOyYK-T3qFn8RK4FoClAqDIWgoO74BX8qs8h79SijeUeAdVtfvGdfprmzpBqutE7i4rKkYyS5HhoRIvVV3LV4vT3Qa3tNc3Aosuvq3DzzI/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h40m34s035.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEheVxhTFqUGUUU6tn92DiJjoLvWaX8dkoTV1FMgk6vNqwkHmcjqmwLt1FcJQrC7ux0RQB31mB3pyGfA-jhUxDOyYK-T3qFn8RK4FoClAqDIWgoO74BX8qs8h79SijeUeAdVtfvGdfprmzpBqutE7i4rKkYyS5HhoRIvVV3LV4vT3Qa3tNc3Aosuvq3DzzI/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h40m34s035.png.jpg)
When you exit, the program lets you boot something else. Let's do something fun.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgD5eT8ktW9-47T_osVAJyFH0PKQy07Wq7heTx4bOtuTdXk-8mLTKZYK5Bx_vjFAq76kdfG8UtkstMGT-vEBRn6OKFZpZfnN2wDSPIr0OakBwh7FUUpDNsTeMSm5DNOV_nzpPf4s6Tqk9vFtlmIQU9X9MFfhocRm8OlpJE9FsN_98mStrW1X42cNu2UUl0/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h43m03s742.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgD5eT8ktW9-47T_osVAJyFH0PKQy07Wq7heTx4bOtuTdXk-8mLTKZYK5Bx_vjFAq76kdfG8UtkstMGT-vEBRn6OKFZpZfnN2wDSPIr0OakBwh7FUUpDNsTeMSm5DNOV_nzpPf4s6Tqk9vFtlmIQU9X9MFfhocRm8OlpJE9FsN_98mStrW1X42cNu2UUl0/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h43m03s742.png.jpg)
Drumroll please!
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi3nHpYpKiYR4IMuEiSa8Tk5MiaWXtlU9Jruq5XEh2M7pqmPsUz2L8tpwb99bdhJLynlGA97BrgrD3jN5_Y8Zx033LnQUXAuVmCrbYslJbDJ8jFGU56oK5-AZwvLWlaAZFmM3g3eivvs-5wyELyJwWQTKHP1DM5s9IV2hP6-A6yRKTZxR2Iw8XK75zP-N4/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h43m18s054.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi3nHpYpKiYR4IMuEiSa8Tk5MiaWXtlU9Jruq5XEh2M7pqmPsUz2L8tpwb99bdhJLynlGA97BrgrD3jN5_Y8Zx033LnQUXAuVmCrbYslJbDJ8jFGU56oK5-AZwvLWlaAZFmM3g3eivvs-5wyELyJwWQTKHP1DM5s9IV2hP6-A6yRKTZxR2Iw8XK75zP-N4/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h43m18s054.png.jpg)
At least a couple games disks were produced for the DECmate family. This is the first volume, with an animated text title screen.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjo-DQPrDxraPX_u6fic7smTfI8WyWWJP_glNvqunifFCI1-nBQjdwqC-2hVv4i0AP3KF_mHz3s3umszoZ_3SR47XnOsaVJGtphoosz32wgTcM5mNX5N4aNnbZIT2xsR_BKPl6QaEHxVcH1vDi9AyO8k_sLjG6eMezuItG9cBAIlD72buPRqbyU0KbDtZw/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h43m35s854.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjo-DQPrDxraPX_u6fic7smTfI8WyWWJP_glNvqunifFCI1-nBQjdwqC-2hVv4i0AP3KF_mHz3s3umszoZ_3SR47XnOsaVJGtphoosz32wgTcM5mNX5N4aNnbZIT2xsR_BKPl6QaEHxVcH1vDi9AyO8k_sLjG6eMezuItG9cBAIlD72buPRqbyU0KbDtZw/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h43m35s854.png.jpg)
I'm not going to go through all of these here on both disks, but we'll show a couple for a taste.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjcNyisLwdxw0z0222CraXSkDexlfZPDi4K_fnfrQwA7LVH3sV6_d7vxMoBO01y_DcIrqWaqY6hbbB5kXQPzPdwKJ2p7fH0m2uL518yUXyO0c8Mm4fnWN5HYpxYbw7d9tWEA0HKmiIirooibJMqqj7ahQRXcSVhPi6zNyAw3ufhru__xQJZt-jxN3sVwew/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h45m07s650.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjcNyisLwdxw0z0222CraXSkDexlfZPDi4K_fnfrQwA7LVH3sV6_d7vxMoBO01y_DcIrqWaqY6hbbB5kXQPzPdwKJ2p7fH0m2uL518yUXyO0c8Mm4fnWN5HYpxYbw7d9tWEA0HKmiIirooibJMqqj7ahQRXcSVhPi6zNyAw3ufhru__xQJZt-jxN3sVwew/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h45m07s650.png.jpg)
The disks also have a "word from your sponsor," soliciting additional programs and encouraging membership in DECUS, the Digital Equipment Corporation Users Society. However, that required talking to your local salesdroid and you know how I hate talking to salesdroids.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEh-KANmPT9AjRx34L9kr6Fv87sLXugJV3piZ-J4RE0a1jHMDKQc_djyZH4jjZm3Dy7LfWRWH9v2CFc71kFtuQIVOG2Cm5Ow3upJ2TuSswYTmW6DEh7oEK8AM7tE1EcRp-FylduTAXietb8uUi1zmLSTrXebu086oGJiVFgRBb-FMmpIpVyrF3evNYH023w/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h47m22s761.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEh-KANmPT9AjRx34L9kr6Fv87sLXugJV3piZ-J4RE0a1jHMDKQc_djyZH4jjZm3Dy7LfWRWH9v2CFc71kFtuQIVOG2Cm5Ow3upJ2TuSswYTmW6DEh7oEK8AM7tE1EcRp-FylduTAXietb8uUi1zmLSTrXebu086oGJiVFgRBb-FMmpIpVyrF3evNYH023w/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h47m22s761.png.jpg)
On this first disk the games are generally simple text-based programs which were (near as I can tell) all or largely written in BASIC, and many likely originated from Digital's own [_101 BASIC Computer Games_](https://archive.org/details/101basiccomputer0000davi/mode/2up), edited by David Ahl (later to found _Creative Computing_). For example, here is the venerable [Hamurabi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hamurabi_%28video_game%29). Compared to the version I have on [6o6's Apple-1 emulator](/2026/03/6o6-v11-faster-6502-on-6502.html), this version is a bit more florid with the text, but uses the same general algorithm as most other spins on Hamurabi and can be "won" (to the extent the game can be) using the same sorts of strategies. Since it's BASIC, you can break out of it and most of these games by pressing Control-C, which will return you to the menu.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEizZ6t1Bj_C9SORzM5n7UIIDqPMqlYMQrTYHZaHxtye_dp3rQJaDzA-N2eGaMfoWiGn0HxkOzXEy8EPZ4n-EyDJX4mG3MW30UYop6k-JSLaSxMY94Qj5PieaQRUsSubUF7MhRSulCDtWUorM2J1v3DJ0__iyP21-JLOtOjacGmikJAnMJjo6PjRACE4bkA/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h49m05s100.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEizZ6t1Bj_C9SORzM5n7UIIDqPMqlYMQrTYHZaHxtye_dp3rQJaDzA-N2eGaMfoWiGn0HxkOzXEy8EPZ4n-EyDJX4mG3MW30UYop6k-JSLaSxMY94Qj5PieaQRUsSubUF7MhRSulCDtWUorM2J1v3DJ0__iyP21-JLOtOjacGmikJAnMJjo6PjRACE4bkA/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h49m05s100.png.jpg)
Defuse puts you in bomb squad mode with your trusty bomb tracker in a suspiciously large governmental building that on paper resembles a Borg cube.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgsyTkssQtMjLmjYnBsyXlQkjfFRZ7EDCnou3e6WQYErP5nZTgb8clpEa3VXmQLfvinA5pxTlgU9qC_9Xx-vo5TW6sWrXw7niwBnC3deY2HN8BFyBENeKEVw5avOadA20dYGfKIcdjFWRyuxmgZVK5FsXjqNWU3Nub2AlpQ2MtcImUWtZlxGB4CNloJV1Q/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h50m06s699.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgsyTkssQtMjLmjYnBsyXlQkjfFRZ7EDCnou3e6WQYErP5nZTgb8clpEa3VXmQLfvinA5pxTlgU9qC_9Xx-vo5TW6sWrXw7niwBnC3deY2HN8BFyBENeKEVw5avOadA20dYGfKIcdjFWRyuxmgZVK5FsXjqNWU3Nub2AlpQ2MtcImUWtZlxGB4CNloJV1Q/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-20h50m06s699.png.jpg)
The game ends up requiring more trigonometry than I was comfortable with, and I decided to take a forced retirement off the 100th floor to see what it would do. Turns out wind resistance is also futile.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEh6eaYLOkQhOKxLiFOxpiCWJq8odAm7NS4QINiLDMVFbsmgXyWKNxyxOCc_Uas__QanHJ1xIiPCDjSGurQXHytHVTYV4FlsaiZpTnrxQayG5TkmTIZu5P2sgR1RaC__sbF3CLkxFUJ5hhuSwXQGF0-KL8-NtFHV4V-13qf_nRCHKic8ghPJ0zPj0xoM3Ak/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-21h02m26s069.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEh6eaYLOkQhOKxLiFOxpiCWJq8odAm7NS4QINiLDMVFbsmgXyWKNxyxOCc_Uas__QanHJ1xIiPCDjSGurQXHytHVTYV4FlsaiZpTnrxQayG5TkmTIZu5P2sgR1RaC__sbF3CLkxFUJ5hhuSwXQGF0-KL8-NtFHV4V-13qf_nRCHKic8ghPJ0zPj0xoM3Ak/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-21h02m26s069.png.jpg)
And what collection of BASIC games would be complete without [Star Trek](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Star_Trek_%281971_video_game%29)?
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjXCLTJcpX6RWMrXaaYrco5XyfA_5tzUTZ-AvGFeFp7pM2xleYdHpqYCtta4gS1TtIu5WvTfF2KPFXxS1l0IAT8PH0xrwCLjmJn1tH5LbvT7KTCM7-Iu2K2h2eq7WK84V46weIis-5LFWC8zKdNHyyiiRw9D3aGk-bfR0cJfDBOx9TJ0t8LZNhxoMDUF9Y/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-21h02m40s462.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEjXCLTJcpX6RWMrXaaYrco5XyfA_5tzUTZ-AvGFeFp7pM2xleYdHpqYCtta4gS1TtIu5WvTfF2KPFXxS1l0IAT8PH0xrwCLjmJn1tH5LbvT7KTCM7-Iu2K2h2eq7WK84V46weIis-5LFWC8zKdNHyyiiRw9D3aGk-bfR0cJfDBOx9TJ0t8LZNhxoMDUF9Y/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-21h02m40s462.png.jpg)
This spin is a modified version of the reworked Super Star Trek version and plays very similarly to other SSTs of the time.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi2hKu161wovW33cdCooz24eSUvRUa2i93R54bg-spM0O2KAHAFsgIB8DDa_cWVqEBLjAzffOmvMpER1WTAMT2_U68dhyphenhyphenXgKRwklp4qZFd3JSXaONrsu67cVyKFqEjBNwsqWu9wvMKP79TffshRutozWmiLWDwhgciM_x4eXG1ug0YklCa1U2nmAEIkH1U/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-21h03m47s630.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi2hKu161wovW33cdCooz24eSUvRUa2i93R54bg-spM0O2KAHAFsgIB8DDa_cWVqEBLjAzffOmvMpER1WTAMT2_U68dhyphenhyphenXgKRwklp4qZFd3JSXaONrsu67cVyKFqEjBNwsqWu9wvMKP79TffshRutozWmiLWDwhgciM_x4eXG1ug0YklCa1U2nmAEIkH1U/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-21h03m47s630.png.jpg)
The second games disk gets a slightly different "digital" logo splash.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEj2Iv4b4hEPSm4fXB7QU-RPwCAE65e34wkqnNjvqmlCQ0gg-DLlTSw2lU1OMjRF2RX5fmdaSCQ8qZuoXq150D89usjIwT6TwSHqCQfP9xE_SKoszhNyMAHX5exTL80sHXKTOuyRAb1vYrWt8YTCKN9V0x9zjFZVYy2VCaC3-rv1CIssFzIItaBITl2JlRY/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-21h04m16s557.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEj2Iv4b4hEPSm4fXB7QU-RPwCAE65e34wkqnNjvqmlCQ0gg-DLlTSw2lU1OMjRF2RX5fmdaSCQ8qZuoXq150D89usjIwT6TwSHqCQfP9xE_SKoszhNyMAHX5exTL80sHXKTOuyRAb1vYrWt8YTCKN9V0x9zjFZVYy2VCaC3-rv1CIssFzIItaBITl2JlRY/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-21h04m16s557.png.jpg)
It also has a more elabourate volcano animation, though one annoyance is I couldn't seem to skip it. However, the "word from our sponsor" screen is the same.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEg507S6DybGjKcnH5ZHyAAyvCAfel3iEJTToqtpxtl2mzRwUNnAuW7OYWONeqqYpi2gib1HSu29YkNtTjUwD1rWC9uxkPauI7cLnp0KZMCyFP4PoAF3JFkAv3kIODWsg2jVmq0Gl4P_YBcVDqQi1X6uhLx1EhWsXBKGEx5qXxRN7TPevAhsndd38pzOAeo/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-21h18m33s348.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEg507S6DybGjKcnH5ZHyAAyvCAfel3iEJTToqtpxtl2mzRwUNnAuW7OYWONeqqYpi2gib1HSu29YkNtTjUwD1rWC9uxkPauI7cLnp0KZMCyFP4PoAF3JFkAv3kIODWsg2jVmq0Gl4P_YBcVDqQi1X6uhLx1EhWsXBKGEx5qXxRN7TPevAhsndd38pzOAeo/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-21h18m33s348.png.jpg)
This disk skews more towards fancier arcade-style games and has a blingier menu (again, sorry for the right column getting cut off). They have the distinct feel of early IBM PC games played on the text screen, and likely because no one custom character set would have sufficed for them all and/or disk space considerations, everything is depicted in the stock text and line-drawing character sets. As before I lack sufficient space to show them all here, but we'll do the highlights.
[![](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi7_xXstzx_kmpGfpUp5Gh2UhhDiKA9cJZUsGQQMBRupYxu_MKCzOZBn_nVP7RFQFl-OogEwh6aYxDqQROwtViOg_m1Gpi_COpQTlTHlIVhI7w68SsxmGWXbloOQXxQ0UwDxHsSysU7cskkW4k75oHGU_iSobaFSdcBwy_nXgRdyV7tnFyVBwn_ILZiccc/s320/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-21h05m05s548.png.jpg)](https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEi7_xXstzx_kmpGfpUp5Gh2UhhDiKA9cJZUsGQQMBRupYxu_MKCzOZBn_nVP7RFQFl-OogEwh6aYxDqQROwtViOg_m1Gpi_COpQTlTHlIVhI7w68SsxmGWXbloOQXxQ0UwDxHsSysU7cskkW4k75oHGU_iSobaFSdcBwy_nXgRdyV7tnFyVBwn_ILZiccc/s1369/crop-vlcsnap-2026-04-21-21h05m05s548.png.jpg)
Did it ever bother you that between all that hard, hard word processing hardness you couldn't just sit back and play Pac-Man on your coffee break? Exceptional Business Solutions, an exceptionally ironic business name for a game title (unrelated to the 2006 company with the same name of indeterminate irony), has you covered with DECmaze. Not DEC _mate_, DEC _maze_. Exceptional Business Solutions was a Culver City, California-based outfit that developed DEC-specific software; this iteration of the name closed up shop in 1993. You will see them again at the end.
